Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY 8. ClonazePAM 0.5 mg PO QAM 9. ClonazePAM 0.25 mg PO BID 10. Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO DAILY 11. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 12. Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg PO DAILY 13. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 2 SPRY NU DAILY 14. Losartan Potassium 100 mg PO DAILY 15. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 16. Potassium Chloride 10 mEq PO DAILY 17. Senna 17.2 mg PO HS constipation 18. Spironolactone 12.5 mg PO DAILY 19. TraZODone 25 mg PO HS 20. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY 21. dextran 70-hypromellose 0.1-0.3 % ophthalmic q2h:PRN dry eyes 22. Glucosamine Sulf-Chondroitin (glucosamine ___ 2KCl-chondroit) 500-400 mg oral qd 23. Torsemide 60 mg PO DAILY 24. Viagra (sildenafil) 100 mg oral prn intercourse
summary: You came to ___ due to fatigue and weakness. You were found to have elevated "troponins" indicative of damage to your heart from a heart attack. You were seen by the interventional cardiologists who determined that performing a cardiac catheterization at this time would have risks that outweigh the potential benefits. For this reason, we kept you on anticoagulation (heparin drip) for 48 hours, and ensured that at the time of discharge you were on an optimal medication regimen for your heart. In addition, due to concern that your stroke symptoms had worsened, you were seen by our neurology experts. They determined that you did not have a new stroke, instead having some temporary worsening of your prior stroke symptoms in the context of your heart attack. These should begin to resolve in the coming weeks. Physical therapy saw you and recommends that you go to rehab, and it is through rehab that you can regain your strength and return to your normal activities.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of HCV cirrhosis on the transplant list, with recent esophageal variceal bleed s/p banding, who presents following 4 episodes of bilious vomiting and BRBPR. # GI Bleed: Appears to be lower in source, as the patient experienced bilious vomiting without evidence of hematemesis. Blood per rectum is also bright red and scant in amount in a hemodynamically stable patient. Her BRBPR resolved in <24 hours. She underwent EGD which revealed persistent esophageal varices and colonoscopy which revealed large hemorrhoids. She was discharged on PPI, carafate, and an aggressive bowel regimen to help avoid constipation. # HCV cirrhosis: Complicated by past variceal bleed. She was continued on her home regimen of lasix, spironolactone, nadalol. Her potassium continued to rise throughout her hospitalization, and as a result, we instructed her to stop taking aldactone for 2 days post-discharge, and then to resume the same dose, but with only once daily dosing. She was discharged with a prescription for an outpatient lab check for ___ prior to meeting with her PCP. # History of opiate abuse: On admission she was not truthful about her current use of suboxone. Upon additional discussion, she revealed that she has been taking suboxone, but does not currently have a provider. It is not clear where she obtains the suboxone that she currently takes. Urine tox screen from admission was positive for opiates and we additionally tested her admission urine specifically for subxone. The result of this test is pending. Of note, the positive result for opiates cannot be explained bu suboxone. She reports that she formerly was prescribed this medication by Dr. ___ but she severed her relationship with that physician ___ long time ago, and has been since intermittently taking suboxone unsupervised. Per discussion with her outpatient pharmacy, the last prescription for suboxone from Dr. ___ was picked up on ___. We placed a social work consult for substance abuse counseling and assistance in finding a new suboxone provider. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression: She was continued on her home regimen of citalopram. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - ___ need enrollment in a suboxone program given her current opiate abuse and unsupervised use of suboxone - Outpatient adjustement of aldactone dosing based on labs and volume status on ___ - Urine suboxone test pending
summary: You were admitted for work-up of your rectal bleeding. You underwent an EGD and a colonoscopy. On EGD you were noted to have varices, one of which was banded. On colonoscopy, you were noted to have large hemorrhoids. Because of the hemorrhoids, we recommend that you take medications to ensure that you avoid becoming constipated. Also, you will need repeat EGD in 2 months to monitor your varices. Also, your potassium has been rising during your stay in the hospital. On discharge, we would like you to not take your aldactone (spironolactone) dose for 2 days. Then, on ___, please restart taking aldactone 100 mg daily (not twice a day).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient Summary: ================= ___ with metastatic gastric cancer (last chemo ___ line taxol/ramicirumab on ___ obstructive uropathy s/p B/L percutaneous nephrostomy tubes, h/o DVT on apixaban, GERD, H. pylori infection, chronic constipation and known hemorrhoids who was admitted for ?pyelonephrosis as well as BRBPR found to be related to hemorrhoids. CT abdomen imaging with findings concerning for possible pyelonephritis. However, patient with stable urinary symptoms for the past few months without fever/chills and hemodynamically stable. She has had multiple prior cultures with with UA with pyuria and bacteria consistent with chronic colonization iso nephrostomy tubes. No antibiotic treament given clinical picture. Had palliative paracentesis and nephrostomy tube replacement while inpatient. # Pyuria # ? pyelonephritis Patient afebrile and well-appearing with chronic urinary symptoms of dysuria,urgency and tenderness at PCN site. UA with pyuria, neg nit and pos leuk and bacteria that was consistent with previous UA. She had multiple previous positive cultures with similar UA that is more consistent with colonization with bacteria iso nephrostomy tubes. She received a dose of ceftriaxone in the ED though would have required Cefepime for appropriate coverage of previous cultures. CT abdomen with evidence of renal stranding concerning for pyelonephritis but given clinical picture, antibiotics were discontinued. She remained afebrile. ___ evaluated nephrostomy tubes without concern for infection. Bilateral nephrostomy tubes replaced ___. Will be due for replacement again in 3 months. # Lower GI bleed # hemorrhoids # constipation BRBPR consistent with previous episodes of hemorrhoids in the setting of significant constipation. Patient only use prune juice at home for management of constipation. GI evaluated in ED and concurs that bleeding consistent with hemorrhoids. CT abd/pelvis without other site of bleed established. Remained HD stable with stable H/H. Started on bowel regimen with Senna, colace, miralax. # metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma # malignant ascites last chemo ___ line taxol/ramicirumab on ___. Awaiting clinical trial appointment at ___ as well as further molecular characterization. She had an appointment for paracentesis on ___ but had an inpatient paracentesis performed for comfort. Pain managed with home regimen of Fentanyl patch and PRN oxycodone. #H/o malignant obstructive uropathy- ___ evaluated nephrostomy tubes in ED. Stable mild pain around nephrostomy tubes without evidence of infection. Will need nephrostomy tube exchanged as outpatient by end of ___. # DVT # Pulmonary embolism: Continued on Eliquis # Hypothyroidism- Continued synthroid # Depression- Continued citalopram and prn Clonazepam. Transitional Issues: ======================== - Nephrostomy tubes exchanged on ___ and due for 3 month exchange by ___. - ___ consider suppressive/prophylactic antibiotics given recurent UTI - Follow up with Dr. ___ for ___ #HCP/Contact: First contact: ___ Second ___ ___ #Code: Full (confirmed)
summary: You had bloody stools for a few days. Your CT abdomen showed concerning signs of a possible kidney infection You had abdominal imaging that showed a lot of abdominal fluid and had the fluid removed during your stay. You were given antibiotics shortly for concern of an infection but since you were not having any new symptoms from your previous burning sensation with urination, antibiotics were stopped. You were evaluated by gastroenterology who believed your bleeding was related to hemorrhoids. You underwent a paracentesis, a procedure where the fluid in your abdomen is removed for comfort. Your nephrostomy tubes were replaced You should continue taking the stool softeners Senna and Docusate daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with PMHx AIDS (CD4: 141; VL less than 20 copies/mL from ___ complicated by multiple OIs including ___ and PCP pneumonia, chronic respiratory failure requiring trach and PEG, and recurrent c. diff, who was admitted with fever, tachypnea, and increased sputum production. Urine culture growing Pseudomonas. Sputum growing multiple GNRs with high levels of antibiotic resistance. He appears significantly improved compared with admission. # Severe Sepsis due to Healthcare Associated Pneumonia vs aspiration pneumonia: Patient presenting with fever, leukocytosis, ___, and lactic acidosis consistent with severe sepsis. Lactate downtrended from 2.9 to 1.1 to 0.9 after 2L IVF in ICU. He was hemodynamically stable while on the floor. His metoprolol was held throughout this admission. His sputum grew out resistant E coli, so he will finish a 2 week course of cefepime. He was seen by the ID service. # Recurrent aspiration: Per his facility staff, he is known to surreptiously sneak food by mouth, and is well known to aspirate. He has also had a lot of oropharyngeal exercises at his facility and his ability to swallow safely without aspirating has NOT improved. Given no improvement with oropharyngeal exercises/rehab and his overall poor health he is unlikely to be able to eat by mouth safely. I discussed with his sister whether he would wish to eat for comfort and accept the risk of aspiration, but she feels that he would want "everything done". He remains NPO and on tube feeds. Continue tube feeds: Jevity 1.5 @ 105ml/hr, infusion time ___ (x20hr/day) to provide 3150kcal and 134gprotein/day. A laryngectomy would enable him to eat safely, but it is a procedure with high associated morbidity and may not be offered to someone in in his poor overall health. - # Recurrent C. diff infection - H/o severe ___. No severe leukocytosis or significant abdominal pain or distention to suggest severe/complicated C. diff infection. Per ID, will continue 125mg PO Vancomycin Q6h and will plan to continue PO vanc at this dose for 7 days following completion of other antibiotics # Tracheostomy problems: stoma site appears large in diameter, but does not appear to be actively infected, but reportedly unable to swallow/phonate. He was seen by ENT in house and they advised that RNs carefully packing between stoma and trach tube (around trach tube) with Kerlex soaked in betadyne, changed once daily, to encourage granulation tissue to form and possibly shrink the diameter of the stoma They hope that this will stimulate him to form his own granulation tissue and that the existing tracheostomy will fit better. They will see him as an outpatient to see if this is working, or if they need to upsize his trach. He is more likely to be able to phonate with a smaller tracheostomy tube. # Acute Kidney Injury: resolved with initial IV fluids and antibiotics. # AIDS: Patient has history of AIDS (multiple OIs); last CD4 was 141 and HIV VL < 20 copies on ___. We continued his home ___ 1 tab daily, Darunavir 800 mg PO DAILY, RiTONAvir 100 mg PO daily and home prophylactic meds: atovaquone and azithromycin. # Chronic abdominal pain: Continued hyoscyamine. # Seizure history: Continued on home Keppra 1250mg PO BID. # Depression/Anxiety/Insomnia: Continued home citalopram, Ativan PRN. # Osteonecrosis / Chronic Right Leg pain: Continued home tylenol for pain. # ? UTI: Urine culture grew out pseudomonas, but patient asyx. The cefepime that is treating the aspiration pneumonia will also cover the UTI. # Hypertension: Metoprolol held throughout hospitalization, and systolic blood pressures in the ___. WIll continue to hold. # Goals of Care: Facility should continue to engage with patient and family regarding his goals of care, especially given his clearly demonstrated urge to eat. I spoke to sister ___ on ___ and called his sister ___ on the day of discharge to review his care.
summary: You were admitted with with shortness of breath, fever, and increased sputum production caused by pneumonia. You were treated with intravenous antibiotics and your pneumonia improved. You also developed an infection in your colon called C. diff colitis, which was treated with oral antibiotics. You were also evaluated by the ENT doctors and they advise packing the area of the stoma around your tracheostomy so that you grow more of your own tissue to hold the trach in place. Also, we feel that it remains unsafe for you to eat unless you are willing to accept the risk of worsened aspiration. You had a PICC line placed so that you can get your iv antibiotics. This should be removed after your antibiotics have been completed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted with concern for acute on chronic cognitive decline and encephalopathy. She was evaluated by neurosurgery, neurology, and psychiatry, all of whom felt that there was no acute process or change in her functioning in corroboration with her family, and she was discharged home to follow-up with her outpatient providers for further management of her depression, cognitive decline/dementia, and thalamic mass. ACTIVE ISSUES # Worsening mental status She presented initially out of concern of a new PCP who felt that she was more severely altered. In the ___ ED, neurosurgery evaluated her who felt that CT imaging done the day of admission at an OSH showed a stable thalamic mass compared to prior imaging, and given stable neurologic exam, there was no need for acute intervention and should follow up in ___ clinic as previously schedled. Neurology also saw her who felt that further neurologic workup should be continued as an outpatient. Psychiatry also evaluated her and felt that there was no psychiatric contraindication to discharge. RPR, TSH, B12 were all negative/normal. A long discussion was had with multiple family members and it was agreed that she was best cared for by her outpatient providers for further evaluation and treatment. # Thalamic mass Stable on imaging as above. Has follow-up already scheduled with the ___ neurosurgery department. CHRONIC ISSUES # Depression She was continued on home doses of sertraline and mirtazapine. # Type II Diabetes Mellitus She was continued on home doses of metformin and aspirin. # Hyperlipidemia She was continued on home dose of simvastatin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Full code
summary: You were admitted to ___ out of concern that you had changes in your mental status from your baseline functioning. You were evaluated by our neurosurgery team who reviewed the images of your brain and saw that the thalamic mass was unchanged for several years and no urgent evaluation was needed. Our neurology colleagues also evlauated you who felt that further neurological workup would best be done by her outpatient providers who know her best. Psychiatry also saw you and agreed that you were safe to discharge and best to follow closely with your outpatient psychiatrists. You were medically stable to leave on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo man with a h/o a-fib on coumadin, dCHF (EF >55% in ___, stroke, stage III CKD and a recent admission in ___ for R foot MSSA osteomyelitis, who presented with ~3 weeks of progressive. ACUTE ISSUES: # acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation Given symptoms (orthopnea, PND and ankle swelling), moderate evidence of volume overload on exam and response to lasix, his SOB was felt to be most consistent with an acute on chronic diastolic CHF exacerbation. Possible contributing factors include dietary changes (increased salt with take-out) and significant Na load in his recent frequent nafcillin infusions. His EKG did not show overt signs of interval ischemic damage and cardiac enzymes were negative x2. He had a CXR, which showed a RLL infiltrate concerning for a pneumonia. In the ED he received levofloxacin, but given that he was not experiencing fever, chills, productive cough, leukocytosis and had preserved oxygen saturation, treatment for pneumonia was discontinued. On discharge, he was instructed to reduce Na/fluids, weigh himself daily and if >3 lb gain, call his PCP. His discharge weight was 94.5kg. He was not discharged on home lasix as he preferred to attempt dietary modifications before starting another pill. Of note, he will need a CXR at ___ weeks to ensure that there is interval resolution of the infiltrate. # Prolonged QTc The patient's QTc was monitored during admission and was consistently 450-470s, notably longer than previous ECGs. Pharmacy was consulted, who felt that contributing medications may include venlafazine and oxycodone, the latter of which is new for him. Of note, his electrolytes were normal. Given his existing heart disease, worsening structural heart disease could not be excluded as a contributing factor. Please trend this closely when administering QTc prolonging agents. # R surgical wound The patient wound was healing well with erythema or purulent drainage to suggest infection. He was given his home equivalent of oxycodone and tylenol. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Atrial Fibrillation The patient was in atrial fibrillation and hemodynamically stable during admission. The patient was continued on diltiazem, carvedilol and warfarin 7.5mg daily. Incidentally, for unclear reasons, he was noted to be sub-therapeutic to 1.6 on discharge, so he was started on a lovenox bridge (100mg bid) and his warfarin was increased to 10mg for 2 days with the plan for him to have his INR checked 2 days after discharge. # CKD Stage 3: The patient's Cr was stable, and 1.6 at time of discharge. # CVA The patient was continued on aspirin. # Depression The patient was continued on venlafaxine. # h/o traumatic seizure The patient was continued on oxcarbazepine. ISSUES OF TRANSITION IN CARE: # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ # ISSUES TO DISCUSS AT FOLLOW UP - diastolic CHF, consider starting lasix depending on weights and symptoms - Repeat CXR in ___ weeks to ensure resolution of RLL infiltrate - orthopedics follow up as scheduled - close follow up with INR. continue lovenox bridge until INR >2. # PENDING STUDIES AT DISCHARGE - ___ BLOOD CX x2
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath that was likely due to an exacerbation of your underlying congestive heart failure, possibly due to increased sodium in your diet and recent IV nafcillin infusions. You received lasix, which caused you to urinate extra fluid, resulting in improvement in your breathing. A chest xray was performed, which showed a possible pneumonia, but given that you were not having fevers, chills, productive cough or requiring oxygen, you were not treated with an extended course of antibiotics, though did receive one dose in the hospital. You will need a repeat chest xray in ___ weeks. Please weigh yourself every morning and record it.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with past medical history notable for HTN, HLD, OSA, PVD, pAF, and chronic back pain who was transferred from ___ with NSTEMI now s/p cath with DES x1. #NSTEMI: Patient had cath ___ with DES to left circ without complication. She was started on clopidogrel 75 mg ___ 81 ___, and high dose atorvastatin. She was also started on a low dose of metoprolol, which was titrated up to 50mg BID over her hospital course. Her troponin trended upward through her hospital stay and peaked at 6 (on ___. We had concern for low clearance versus an ischemic process. She remained without symptoms, her ECG did not show any ischemia, and her trop down-trended on the day of discharge. Please follow-up this elevated troponin especially in light of her asymptomatic NSTEMI. #Chronic Paroxysmal Afib: Patient had episodes of afib with concurrent ST depressions in the anterior leads. She spontaneously converted back to sinus rhythm. Patient was started on Coumadin 2.5 daily with lovenox bridging until she was therapeutic. She will need her INR drawn on ___ with close follow-up. #Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Patient presented with peripheral edema, exertional dyspnea, elevated BNP, and pulmonary edema on CXR, which is most likely in the setting on known HCTZ non-compliance. Patient received several doses of Lasix while inpatient and pulmonary edema was resolved. Please consider restarting diuretic as outpatient if needed. (Patient has been euvolemic off diuretic for last 4 days.) The patient's episodes of afib may have also contributed to the transient heart failure. #Nausea with intermittent diarrhea and constipation: Patient presented to the ___ with complaints of nausea and diarrhea, but this resolved upon admission to the floor. Patients states that this is a chronic problem. She had a KUB, which showed a non-obstructive bowel gas pattern and stool throughout the colon. Her symptoms are most likely consistent with IBS. #HTN: Patient was continued on her home dose of valsartan. #Depression: Patient was continued on home dose of sertraline. #OSA: Patient was continued on her home CPAP. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Medication changes [ ] Aspirin 81 mg and Plavix 75 mg were started s/p cath; these need to be continued for at least ___ year after discharge [ ] Patient was started on metoprolol succinate 50mg BID for afib rate control. We considered rhythm control with amiodarone, but held off for now given she is asymptomatic and her LFTs were mildly elevated upon discharge. She remained in sinus rhythm for the last two nights. [ ] Warfarin was started with lovenox bridging. Her INR was therapeutic at time of discharge at 2.5. She will need follow up in ___ clinic. [ ] Next INR to be drawn on ___ by ___ and sent to ___ until follow up with ___ clinic. [ ] Patient's LFTs were noted to be elevated during admission. They down-trended over the hospital course, but the etiology of these elevations should be followed up [ ] Please evaluate patient for heart failure and restart diuretic as needed. She was non-compliant with thiazide. (She was treated effectively in hospital with IV Lasix) and so it was held on discharge. Discharge labs: Cr: 0.6 INR: 2.5 Trop T: 5.37 CK-MB: 2 Weight: 66.2 kg
summary: You presented to ___ because you had nausea and chest pressure. A procedure was performed to look at your heart arteries, and we found a blockage in one of them. A stent was placed to keep the blood vessel open. You also had fluid in your lungs when you came to the hospital. We gave you medicine and you began to feel better. You were started on aspirin 81 mg and clopidogrel (also known as Plavix) 75 mg. It is very important that you continue these medicines after you leave the hospital for at least one year, unless your cardiologist instructs you otherwise. If you don't take these medications consistently, you will be at risk of a heart attack.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Hypoxia: Admitted recently to an OSH on ___ after being found to be hypoxic by ___ services. CTA was done during that time and was negative for PE. Her symptoms resolved after use of nebulizers. During this admission as well, the patient's symptoms resolved with respiratory treatments. She was initiated on ___ 500/50 (equivalent dose of previous fluticasone prescription) and given duonebs q6h. CT-A chest was done to look for new pathology to explain her symptoms. There was no evidence of clot or other finding to explain her symptoms. After two days of ___, she was satting well on room air, and maintained her O2 saturation with exercise in the hallway. Thus she is discharged on ___, scheduled albuterol nebs, spiriva and theophylline. There was some concern for theophylline toxicity given hypotension, but her symptoms improved with fluids, and therefore she was restarted on this. Her theophylline level returned at < 2.5. # Hypotension: Attributed to poor PO intake; improved with IVF. Doxazosin was held while inpatient and was eventually discontinued. The patient received 500cc IVF prior to discharge. She had no evidence of shock on admission, was ruled out for MI, and had normal lactate. She will be living with one of her daughters, who knows to ensure that she drinks enough fluids/PO intake. Mirtazapine was also started during this admission, which will help her PO intake. Her nifedepine dose was decreased prior to discharge as well. BPs 120-130s after initial fluid bolus in ED. # ___: Attributed to pre-renal azotemia (Cr=1.5 at admission) that improved with IV fluids back to baseline (Cr=1.0 upon discharge).. # Decreased PO intake: Multifactorial including potential depression with death of husband, malignancy and its treatment. Of note, the partial esophageal obstruction does not seem to be causing much decline in PO intake, as the patient denies dysphagia or odynophagia. It is really that she has no "interest in food" anymore. She was started on mirtazapine 7.5 mg QHS while inpatient. # Esophageal cancer: CT chest revealed partial esophageal blockage. Asymptomatic except with large pills. ___ require therapy with radiation or esophageal stenting in future. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== - home ___ and physical therapy - adequate PO intake - will be living with daughter - ongoing HTN ___ (decreased nifedipine to 60 mg ER, d/c'd doxazosin) - consideration of further cancer ___ with Dr. ___ - ___, Spiriva, theophylline, scheduled albuterol nebs for respiratory management
summary: You came in with low blood pressure, oxygen requirement and worsening functioning of your kidneys. The blood pressure and kidney issue were from not drinking and eating enough. For your oxygen requirement we made sure there was no clot in your lungs, and gave you a new lung medication called ___. This, along with scheduled nebulizers, made it such that you were breathing well without requiring oxygen. When we were looking for clot, we found a worsened blockage of your esophagus from your cancer. This was not causing you any problems with eating or drinking, but may need to be addressed in the future. Please continue to eat and drink as much as possible. To limit the risk of low blood pressure, we are decreasing your dose of nifedepine and stopping your doxazosin (which you did not receive while in the hospital).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ woman with h/o AF on digoxin, bilateral ___ edema, osteoporosis, and bilateral knee replacements who presented to ___ after fall with head trauma and diagnosed with SDH (seen by Neurosurgery at ___ upon arrival with repeat non-contrast head CT showing stable SDH), admitted to Medicine for syncope work-up, likely due to bradycardia while on digoxin vs atrial fibrillation. ACTIVE ISSUES # Syncope: likely due to bradycardia while on digoxin vs AF. Other etiologies considered were cardiac (structural vs arrhythmia), orthostatic hypotension from hypovolemia or autonomic dysfunction, vasovagal, or neurogenic. Suddenness of syncope with no prodrome made cardiac etiology more likely than vasovagal. If her AF is not well-controlled on digoxin, her AF could directly be causing her syncope. Structural abnormality possible but recent TTE in ___ showed no significant abnormalities. Orthostatic hypotension from autonomic dysfunction less likely given no history of diabetes or other neurologic disease. Hypovolemia induced orthostatic hypotension a possibility given patient's thirst and physical exam findings of being fluid down. Neurogenic less likey given no other focal or systemic neurologic findings. Patient was encouraged to take PO liquid and never required IVF to maintain blood pressures. Patient monitored on telemetry throughout admission and HR stable in ___. Digoxin was held due to concern for causing bradycardia down to the ___ and patient was started on metoprolol tartrate 12.5mg BID for rate control of AF and then decreased down to metoprolol succinate 12.5mg QD out of concern for bradycardia. Physical therapy was consulted and recommended rehab in order to reach functional level to care for herself and her husband. - Continue metoprolol succinate 12.5mg QD (first dose should be evening of ___ - Monitor blood pressures # Subdural hematoma: secondary to head trauma from fall due to syncopal episode. CT at ___ and repeat at ___ show stable bleed. No focal neurologic signs of cerebral herniation and no signs of midline shift on CT. Neurological exam benign and unchanged with no focal deficits. Held anticoagulation until HD 3, when neurosurgery recommended it was safe to restart heparin prophylaxis. No change in neuro exam. - Restart home dose ASA 81mg - Serial neuro exams # Atrial Fibrillation: no RVR documented, rate-controlled on digoxin at home, CHADS2 = 1 on ASA 81mg, hemodynamically stable. No need for cardioversion given hemodynamic stability. Held ASA given contraindication for anticoagulation due to SDH on head CT. Held digoxin given concern for bradycardia-induced syncope. Started on metoprolol tartrate 12.5mg BID for rate control of AF and then decreased down to metoprolol succinate 12.5mg QD out of concern for bradycardia. Per neurosurgery, it is safe to restart anticoagulation as needed. - Continue metoprolol succinate 12.5mg QD (first dose should be evening of ___ - Restart home dose ASA 81mg CHRONIC ISSUES # Lower extremity edema: chronic, no edema apparent currently - Continue on home furosemide, potassium chloride as needed # Depression: chronic, stable - Continue on home fluoxetine # Osteoporosis: chorinc, stable - Continue on home calcium supplement # Transitional Issues: Rehab with physical therapy recommended in order to reach prior functional status to take care of both herself and her husband with dementia, due to general deconditioning s/p syncope with fall. - Follow-up with Dr. ___ subdural hematoma and schedule head CT w/o contract before appointment - Follow-up with PCP regarding need for furosemide/potassium given lack of leg edema during hospital stay - Follow-up with PCP regarding metoprolol dose for rate control of AF
summary: You were admitted to ___ after a fall, found to have bleeding in your brain called a subdural hematoma. Your fall was likely caused by a very low heart rate, which was likely caused by digoxin (your atrial fibrillation medication). We discontinued this medication and started ___ on a low dose medication called metoprolol. This may also lower your heart rate, so we used a very low dose. We also stopped your furosemide (lasix) because ___ did not have evidence of heart failure while you were here. ___ should talk to your primary care doctor about whether to restart this. ___ did not require surgery for your subdural hematoma.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ F with hx DM, hypothyroidism, anxiety, HTN and Bell's palsy who presented with fevers, headache, neck stiffness Differential diagnosis on admission included bacterial, viral and aseptic meningitis in the setting of subjective fevers, heachache and neck stiffness. An admission LP was notable for 0 WBCs, 101 RBCs, 45 prot, 126 glucose lowering suspicion for an infectious meningitis as an etiology to symptoms and antibiotics were discontinued. In the setting of transient weakness was seen by neurology in the ED. CT head in the ED negative and recent MRI (at ___) ___. Continued on home Synthroid # Depression/anxiety. Continued on Fluoxetine # Hyperlipidemia. Continued on home Rosuvastatin
summary: You were admitted for headaches and left sided numbness. You also have Bells palsy. We tested you for Lyme disease, syphillis, and HSV as possible causes for your headaches and neurologic symptoms. We performed a lumbar puncture to see if there was an infection in your brain or in the fluid that surrounds your brain. Our tests looking for an infectious cause of your symptoms were all negative. We are still waiting on some results to return but we do not think you have an infection. We will contact you with any concerning results that come back in the future. You were treated with antibiotics that were stopped as your symptoms improved. You were evaluated by a neurologist who thought you had a type of headache called occipital neuralgia.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with a history of hypertension and depression who presented with SDH/SAH after fall potentially secondary to syncope. Serial CT scans of the head were stable, and neurosurgery determined that there was no acute surgical intervention indicated. #Subdural Hemorrhage/Subarachnoid Hemorrhage #Fall #Syncope She has no memory of falling or hitting her head. CT at OSH revealed right frontal SDH and SAH. Repeat CT at ___ showed stable bleed. Neurosurgery evaluated the patient and determined that no acute surgical intervention was indicated. Orthostatic vital signs negative. Telemetry without arrhythmia. Echocardiogram showed EF of 60% with no intracardiac cause of syncope identified. Physical therapy was consulted and recommended discharge to rehab. She was given seizure prophylaxis with Keppra 500mg PO BID x 7-days. Day 1 = ___. Day 7 = ___. Patient should follow-up with the Concussion Clinic. #Dizziness/Vertigo Patient reporting worsening dizziness/vertigo since her fall. Neurology was consulted and determined that the etiology of central vs. peripheral cannot be determined at this time. MRI of brain could be obtained to determine etiology, but would not change management. She was started on meclizine BID. This was uptitrated to TID, and helped with her dizziness. This should be given PRN as standing doses can worsen symptoms. #Hyponatremia Patient with mild hyponatremia to 131. Urine lytes suggestive of SIADH. She was maintained on a 1L fluid restriction. Please continue to monitor. #Nausea #Nutrition Patient reports that she has not been able to eat food for over ___ years without vomiting. She has been living on Ensure shakes and low sodium V8 drinks. She should have outpatient GI Follow-up. #Depression #SI without plan Psych was consulted in the ED and did not feel that she needed to have a ___. TSH/B12 normal. Collateral from the patient's brothers was that the patient has been suffering from a depressed mood for many years due to chronic back pain. She does not believe in western medicine and did not want help for her pain. Throughout the hospitalization, she asked for "a pill to end it all." Psychiatry recommended holding inpatient initiation of antidepressant medication at this time. Social work was consulted. #Constipation Patient has baseline constipation that requires manual disimpaction. This may be related to her poor PO intake She had a standing bowel reg. #Hypertension SBP on presentation was as high as SBPs in 240's. She was initially on a nicardapine drip, which was discontinued and her pressures have remained better controlled. She was started on amlodipine 5mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================= - Patient should follow-up with the Concussion Clinic. - Upon discharge, will need outpatient ___ rehab. Please make sure this is arranged prior to discharge. - Outpatient GI follow-up for persistant nausea after eating - Patient should establish care with primary care doctor - Patient with signs of depression. Consider outpatient initiation of SSRI. - Discharged on amlodipine 5mg daily for hypertension. Uptitrate as needed. - Continue meclizine on PRN basis. Standing meclizine can worsen symptoms.
summary: You were hospitalized at ___. You came to the hospital after a fall. You were found to have a bleed in your brain. You had two separate CT scans of your brain, which showed that the brain bleed was stable. The neurosurgery team did not think you needed surgery since the bleed was stable. You were very dizzy after your fall. We consulted the neurology team, who determined that the brain bleed was causing the dizziness. We gave you medication for the dizziness. This should slowly improve as your body gets rid of the blood in your brain. It is very important that you establish care with a primary care doctor after you leave rehab. It may also help for you to follow-up in the concussion clinic and in the ___ rehab clinic for your dizziness. Please continue to work with physical therapy to get stronger.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ PCKD, stage III NSCLC with brain mets s/p 4 cycles of Altima/Cisplatin, s/p cyberknife, with recent discontinuation of crizotinib and recent start of ___ ___ who presents with RUQ and right flank pain found to have 5mm nonobstructing kidney stone (stone present on imaging from ___ as well). Most likely this is actually cystic pain due her underlying cystic liver/kidney disease. Not thought to be an infected stone by urology. Pt's initially had pyuria with urine culture growing vaginal flora. Treated with ciprofloxacin x 7 days to complete on ___. Pain control with MS ___. ___ improved with IVF and good PO intake (1.8 -> 1.3). ============= ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # UTI -Pt initially had fever (to 101 in ED) and positive UA, although there was no obstruction/hydro on imaging, and urine culture grows vaginal organisms unlikely to cause significant disease. However, treated on basis of fever and pyuria with 7 day course of ciprofloxacin ___. #nonobstructive nephrolithiasis: Pt noted to have 5mm nonobstructing stone in lower pole of right kidney. As per urology, as the stone is nonobstructing, it is unlikely to be causing pain, and there is no acute intervention other than fluids, which she received. # Acute Kidney Injury - likely ___ very poor PO intake as she has been struggling with this in the setting of cancer treatments. GI adverse effects with ___ may also be contributing. She has been admitted before with ___ due to volume depletion. In absence of hydronephrosis/obstruction on imaging, the kidney stone is not likely the source of the elevated creatinine. Pt was treated with fluids with improvement in Creatinine from 1.8 to 1.3. #Right flank pain: Pt reported severe right flank pain. Unlikely due to nonobstructing stone, and ultrasound did not show hydronephrosis. GIven her extensive polycystic liver and kidney disease, a ruptured or infected cyst may be the etiology of her pain. She was treated with morphine and was discharged on a home PO morphine regimen. #stage III NSCLC with brain mets - s/p chemotherapy and most recently progression on crizotinib now on ___. Known brain mets. Pt stated during this admission that she did not want to continue with ___ due to adverse effects. She discussed her plans with outpatient oncologist and decision was made to defer treatment for 2 weeks and reassess at next outpatient visit. She would likely benefit from a lower dose. #Coagulopathy: likely in the setting of poor PO intake. ============= CHRONIC ISSUES ============= # Hypothyroidism - continued levothyroxine # Depression - contined sertraline ============= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ============= - Will need monitoring of her Cr given ___ on presentation - Will need to complete 7 day course of Cipro for UTI (to complete on ___ - Will need to follow up with Dr. ___ further chemotherapy and pain control (started MS ___ on this admission)
summary: You were admitted due to pain in your right flank. You were found to have a small (5mm) kidney stone lodged in your right kidney, which did not appear to be causing any obsruction or blockage. You were seen by the Urology team and they felt that this was unlikely to be the cause of your pain and did not feel that you would benefit from further Urologic interventions. You had a fever so we treated you with antibiotics. You should complete a seven day course of Cipro, which should end on ___. You were also dehydrated and had an elevation in your creatinine (1.8), which was 1.3 on your day of discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ male with PMH major depression presenting with worsening tremor, altered mental status, gait ataxia, lithium toxicity. . ACTIVE ISSUES ============== #. Lithium toxicity: Patient admitted hyperreflexic with confusion ataxia and tremor. His wife stated that two weeks ago, she had run out of lithium 150mg tablets and had been giving him lithium 300mg 3x daily rather than lithium 300mg 2x daily and lithium 150mg at night. Admission lithium level was 2.5. Head CT was negative for acute process. He was evaluated by toxicology who recommended aggressive hydration. Dialysis was not pursued as the patient appeared stable and lithium level trended down in the first 12 hours of his stay from 2.5 to 2.1. Aggressive hydration was contiuned and lithium level normalized. He remained confused with tremor and MRI was obtained to rule out stroke (given history of "small strokes" according to the wife). MRI was negative for acute or recent stroke and showed only degenerative changes. Toxicology believed that the clinical exam and mental status lags behind the serum lithium level and recommended continued intervenous fluids. With time, tremor resolved, gait instability improved. His confusion improved but did not resolve completely. ___ believed that he would need 24 horu supervision for ADLs. His wife wanted to assume the role of caregiver. He was discharged home with the expectation that his mental status will continue to improve over the course of the next ___ days. His outpatient psychiatrist, Dr. ___ was contacted who agreed with discontinuation of lithium and followup with her. . Bipolar disorder: Lithium was discontinued as above. Venlafaxine dose was confirmed with family/psych/pharmacy as Venlafaxine 200mg immediate release QAM, 250mg QPM which is above the max dose of 400mg daily. Outpatient psychiatrist recommended lowering dose to Venlafaxine 200mg BID. # Tension Headaches: Per patient's outpatient psychiatrist, he takes topirimate for headache. Discontinued topirimate according to outpatient Psychiatrist's request. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #. Diabetes: continued home insulin regimen. . #. Hypothyroid: Continued levothyroxine. . #. Neuropathy: Continued gabapentin. . #. GERD: Continued omeprazole. . #. Depression: Continue venlafaxine. . #. Tremor: Continued propanolol. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============== - Followup with psychaitrist and PCP - ___ mental status, tremor and gait for improvement. CODE STATUS: FULL Contact information:Wife ___ ___ on Admission: Topamax 25 Mg 1 po qhs Gabapentin 800 Mg 8 1 po tid Venlafaxine Hcl 100 Mg ___ in am and 2 in pm Lithium Carbonate 300 QAM and Qnoon Lithium Carbonate 150 Mg 1 po qd Propranolol Hcl 40 Mg 1 po tid Synthroid ___ Mcg take 1 tablet (150MCG) by oral route on 6 days per week and 1 & ___ tab on ___ Lantus 100 Unit/ml for injections 12 QAM, 20QPM Humalog sliding scale Prilosec 20 Mg 1 time per day
summary: As you know, you were admitted for tremor and confusion. We checked your blood work and found that your lithium level was very high. The Toxicology team was consulted who recommended treatment with agressive fluid hydration and close monitoring. We performed a CT scan of the head and an MRI of the head which did not show anything concerning. With time, your tremor and confusion improved. Though you have not completely returned to your baseline level of functioning, we expect that this will improve over the course of the next 7 to 10 days. We discussed your medications with Dr. ___ agreed that we should stop lithium and decrease venlafaxine. she did not think that topirimate was necessary any more either.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ man with HCV cirrhosis c/b HCC s/p ___, HTN, HLD, T2DM (insulin-dependent), CAD s/ CABG (___), and h/o nephrolithiasis who is presenting with 6 days of intermittent and severe R flank pain with radiation to R testicle, found to have non-obstructing nephrolithiasis and constipation on imaging. # R flank pain # R testicular pain Pt reports having 6d history of intermittent, severe R flank pain and R testicular pain. Labs notable for mild leukocytosis with neutrophilic predominance, +trace hematuria, and CT with non-obstructing nephrolithiasis and constipation. RUQUS reassuring. Scrotal U/S without acute findings, however did not intratesticular cystic lesion w/o concerning features. Overall most concerning for symptomatic nephrolithiasis with contribution from constipation as well. DDx also includes epididymitis however U/A non-infection appearing, GC Chlamydia negative, and mild leukocytosis resolved w/o antibiotics and thus more likely representative of a reactive leukocytosis to pain. He was started on Tamsulosin to assist in passing stone. He was also hydrated, and received home tramadol prn, Tylenol for pain control. He was started on Miralax and Dulcolax for constipation. Per pt, he has a h/o nephroliathiasis in the past and previous stone was analyzed; however, he cannot recall the results. We advised pt to save stone if possible for analysis. Pain stabilized prior to discharge. # HCV cirrhosis s/p DDLT (___) LFTs stable, normal. Tacrolimus level prior to discharge: 3.3 - tacro dosing at discharge: 1.5mg BID CHRONIC ISSUES ================ # HTN # HLD # CAD s/p CABG (___) Continued home Metoprolol 100mg BID, Losartan 25mg daily, Pravastatin 40mg qPM, and Aspirin 81mg qd # CKD, Stage III Baseline Cr ~1.3-1.4. Monitored BMP daily. # T2DM, insulin-dependent Continued home Lantus/NPH + ISS. # Insomnia Continued home Trazodone 100mg QHS PRN # Depression Continued home Bupropion 300mg qd # CODE: Presumed FULL TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Advised pt to try to save renal stone if this passes. If right flank pain does not improve, consider Urology referral. [] Advised patient to limit consumption of red meat and nuts, as well as to stay well hydrated [] F/up pending UCx [] CT scan notable for: cortical deformity of the posterior left eleventh rib is likely chronic but is new since ___. No L. sided rib pain, however consider work-up and tx for possible osteoporosis. [] Ensure pt has regular bowel movements - CT scan showed moderate stool burden. Was started on daily Miralax and Bisacodyl.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had abdominal pain and right sided back pain. We suspect this pain was from constipation and kidney stones, which we saw on a CAT scan. You received fluids and were started on a medication called Flomax to help you pass these stones. You received Tramadol for pain. You improved and were ready to leave the hospital. Please stay well hydrated, as this will help with passing the kidney stones. Please limit the amount of red meat and nuts you eat, as this will help prevent kidney stone formation. Please try to strain your urine and save any stones that you pass. Please bring the stone into your primary care doctor's office so that they can send it off for analysis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with history of morbid obesity, non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, systolic HF (EF 15% s/p ICD), and OSA, who presented on ___ with Vtach s/p ICD firing with worsening lactic acidosis. Patient noted to be volume overloaded on exam and was started on bumex drip. He was ultimately diuresed approximately 30 liters over the course of the admission. Additional therapies included afterload reduction with maximal doses of hydralazine and isordil as well as the addition of digoxin to improve contractility. A swan-ganz catheter was placed for tailored-therapy monitoring. Due to a persistently low cardiac output and episodes of symptomatic hypotension, the patient required IV inotrope therapy with dobutamine initially at 5 mcg/kg/min. As the patient was diuresed and adjustments were made to his other oral medications, several attempts were made to wean the patient off of the dobutamine drip. These attempts were unsuccessful as reflected by worsening hemodynamic parameters measured via the ___, worsening lactic acidosis, episodes of hypotension, and subjective discomfort reported by Mr. ___. Several meetings were held with Mr. ___ involving the primary team, social work, and palliative care to discuss the limited options for his end stage heart failure and to address goals of care given his overall poor prognosis. It was once again determined that he would not be a candidate for transplant or a ventricular assist device. The patient opted to become DNR/DNI but decided to not turn off the ICD. Mr ___ decided that he would prefer to be discharged home with ___ services rather than go to a ___ facility. He was discharged on dobutamine at a rate of 3.5 mcg/kg/min indefinitely. He will require daily ___ care and close follow up with the heart failure team. Discharge weight was 154.3kg. Discharge Bumex 4mg QPM, 5mg Daily # Acute decompensated HF c/b cardiogenic shock: pt w/ hx sHF and NICM EF 15%: patient volume overloaded on exam w/ elevated BNP and pulmonary edema as well as lactic acidosis. PA catheter placed for tailored therapy with decreased MvO2 and elevated PCWP c/w cardiogenic shock. Patient started on dobutamine for ionotropic support. Afterload reduction achieved with nipride gtt which was transitioned to captopril. Pt diuresed with bumex gtt w/ diuril IV boluses. Spironolactone increased to 200 BID in setting of hypokalemia. Home metoprolol initially held. Patient monitored with daily weights, strict I/Os with ___ cc fluid restriction, 2g sodium diet. Patient was stabililized on dobutamine and continued bumex gtt. Attempts made to wean the dobutamine were unsuccessful, prompting the addition of hydralazine/imdur along with digoxin to allow a very slow wean of the dobutamine, which ultimately could be weaned only to 3.5. Discharged on Bumex 4mg QPM 5 mg daily. # ICD firing: Patient presents after feeling ICD firing. Notably he had a prior admission a week prior where he had VT, thought to be in the setting of electrolyte abnormalities. Pacer interrogated and had one shock day prior to admission. Mg was 1.4 on admission and K to 3.7 though he claims he did not miss any doses of his home supplemenation. EP consulted and recommened continuing amiodarone 400 BID and he was ultimately decreased to requiring 400 mg QD on discharge. # Anxiety/depression: home sertraline continued. SW consulted- goals of care conversations as above. # TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - needs CPAP at home -considered for CRT for cardiomyopathy. QRS is widened but not >120. non ischemic. -CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ -CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI, keep ICD on for now
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for management of your severe heart failure. Unfortunately, your heart was not pumping blood well enough to meet the demands of your body. We placed a monitor in your neck to measure important information about your heart as we made changes to your medications to find the best regimen. Several therapies were tried. First, we gave you medicine to remove the excess fluid from your body. We also added or increased the dose of several other medications. During your stay, you required a continuous IV medication called dobutamine to assist your heart's ability to pump blood. Despite all the changes we tried, you were not able to come off dobutamine. You will need to remain on this medication indefinitely. In order to help with your care at home, you will have a visiting nurse to assist with all medications and other therapies.

text: Brief Hospital Course: CORONARIES/Chest pain: Differential is broad. On repeat catheterization, appears D2 subtotal occlusion is recanalizing. The occlusion may have been embolic, as patient was found to have a PFO on echocardiogram, confirmed on TEE with bubble study. Other possibilites include coronary vasospasm, as well as gastroesphogeal reflux and oesphageal spasm. Patient was started on warfarin anticoagulation with exoxaparin bridge, as well as anti-platelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel for at least for 1 month. For the vasospasm, she was started on amlodipine and atenolol. She was also advised to follow-up with outpatient GI for workup of esophageal spasm and management of GERD (has ___ on recent EGD). Hypothyroidism: TSH high, T4 normal, most likely sick euthyroid. Will continue levothyroxine. Patient should follow up with primary care physician as an outpatient to repeat TSH and free T4. ___ Esophagus: Continued omeprazole. Migraine Headaches: Will continue topomax, but will hold triptans in the setting of questionable vasospasm. Depression/Anxiety: Will continue citalopram and PRN lorazepam.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation of recurrent chest pain. You were found to have a small hole between the two sides of your heart. It was thought that a clot may have travelled through this hole and caused an obstruction in one of your coronary arteries. You were treated with medication to alleviate the symptoms. You had an echocardiogram, cardiac catheterization and EKG's to come to this diagnosis. You were started on a blood-thinner called warfarin. The dose of this has to be adjusted based on your blood levels. You have been given a prescription for the lab work, which has to be drawn every ___, and ___. These results will be faxed to your primary care, Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with history of HTN, HLD, CKD with long-standing history of SVT who was transferred from ___ for definitive treatment of both atrial fibrillation + atrial flutter. He originally presented with a wide-complex tachycardia, which s/p interventions e.g. adenosine, transitioned into an SVT/atrial flutter and fib. # Atrial fibrillation + atrial flutter: Patient was in sinus rhythm the morning prior to transfer on a Diltiazem gtt. He was in A-fib upon arrival to our floor. He was continued on a Diltiazem gtt overnight (as well as a heparin gtt for anti-coagulation), and again converted into sinus rhythm. He was originally scheduled for both a cardioversion and subsequent ablation for a-fib; however, as he was in sinus rhythm, only the ablation was conducted. He underwent a successful ablation for atrial flutter, and of note was in significant atrial-fibrillation during the procedure. He was loaded with ibutilide in the EP lab. Post-procedure, he was started on Apixaban for anti-coagulation and Flecainide for increased rhythm control. He should continue on Flecainide 100 mg PO BID, and Apixaban 5 mg PO BID. He was given a 30-day supply of Apixaban through ___, as his pre-authorization for the medication was not approved. Alternatives to Apixaban should be discussed at his outpatient PCP and ___ appointments. # HTN: Patient remained normotensive throughout his stay. He was continued on his home Clonidine 0.2 mg PO BID and Amlodipine 5 mg PO qday. His previous home dose of Metoprolol was XL 50 mg PO qday; this was lowered to 25 mg PO qday due to increased rhythm control for A-fib with Flecainide as well. # HLD: Patient was continued on his home Pravastatin 40 mg PO qPM and Aspirin 81 mg PO qday (which was decreased from his previous home dose of 324mg PO qday). He was also continued on his home Fish Oil tablets. # CKD: Baseline 1.5 to 1.7 in ___. Creatinine in-house was 1.3-1.5. Nephrotoxins were avoided, and his medications were renally dosed. # Anxiety/Depression: Patient was continued on his home Sertraline 100 mg PO qday. He was also given Lorazepam (in-house only) instead of his home Alprazolam PRN anxiety. # Chronic shoulder pain: Patient was continued on his home Gabapentin 300 mg PO BID and Tramadol 50 mg PO BID this admission. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient is to return on ___ for ___ of ___ monitor pick-up. - Patient was noted to have sleep apnea this admission; a sleep study should be pursued as outpatient (this may help in order to manage his A-fib). - Of note, patient was originally scheduled to undergo cardioversion for A-fib and subsequent A-flutter ablation; however, the patient was in sinus rhythm immediately pre-procedure, so underwent the ablation only. He was in significant A-fib during the ablation procedure, and was loaded with Ibutilide in the lab prior to coming to the floor. - Patient was started on Flecainide and Apixaban (prior auth approved only for 3 days, may need warfarin if not approved) this admission. - His home Metoprolol XL dose was decreased from 50mg to 25mg daily. - Patient will follow-up closely with his PCP as well as Dr. ___.
summary: You were admitted with palpitations and a very fast heart rate, and you were transferred from another hospital for procedures to prevent your heart rate from remaining so high. Your heart rates were originally controlled on an IV drip. You had an ablation, which was a procedure to eliminate one of your abnormal heart rhythms. The procedure was successful, and you tolerated it well. Your heart still has another underlying fast rhythm, which we are controlling with medications. You were started on Flecainide, in order to help control your rhythm, and Apixaban (anticoagulant) to help keep your blood thin while the rhythm is being controlled. Your home Metoprolol was continued, but at a lower dose than before. The apixaban has been approved only for 3 days by ___ and you were given a 30-day free trial from ___. Please discuss with your insurance company about whether they can approve apixaban.

text: Brief Hospital Course: imaging: CT chest ___ Minimal ground-glass opacity in the right upper lobe potentially reflecting infection, most likely viral or atypical, although the finding is relatively mild and if other sources of infection are more likely, they should be assessed as such. CT abd/pelvis ___ (pt refused oral contrast after a few sips) 1. Limited evaluation without intravenous contrast. No evidence of intra-abdominal infection. Active # Myeloma: D+20 today. Melphalan D-3, D-2 completed. Reinfusion completed ___, received 14.275x10^6/kg CD34+ cells. # fever of unknown origin: Febrile overnight ___, then briefly yesterday AM although not sustained. - CT sinus negative, continue afrin - LUE u/s negative - removed central line, culture tip pend - pan CT looking for occult source of infection unrevealing, although potential GGO in RUL could be source. described as mild in final read. patient asymptomatic, non neutropenic, neg lung exam. if persistent fevers, consider reimaging and treating, otherwise likely resolved infection. - viral studies: CMV, EBV, HHV6, adenovirus pending - culture urine pending # Nausea: will simplify regimen to see what is working. - ativan or compazine prn for nausea, nurses ___ try to give pre-meals and pre-meds - dex and emend today (no IV access, so will give 3d course po emend) - radiology unable to comment on appearance of stomach on CT since very little po contrast taken in by pt, but no obstruction seen. Does have lg hiatus hernia on prior EGD # Fluid volume overload: improved, monitor weight daily. # Deconditioning. ___ following. weakness since admission. Chronic: # Neuropathy: To continue oxycodone prn, and gabapentin, changed to 600mg TID from 900mg BID secondary to sedation with fent PCA. # HTN: To continue diltiazem, lisinopril inpatient, will hold ASA throughout admission. Do not restart until told to by Dr ___ as outpatient. # Post menopausal symptoms: Patient states she takes prempro as needed at home for relief of acute onset hot flashes/perspirations. She usually takes this on a 2x weekly basis, d/c and will monitor for symptoms. # Depression: Sertraline 50mg daily. Long discussion today regarding life stressors over 30min.
