ANC engraftment date ___. 2 unit plt most recently ___, no pRBC required. Platelets normal today. #Febrile neutropenia. Resolved. # LLL pneumonia: completed levaquin x 7 days for LLL PNA ___. # Mucositis: Resolved. # R knee discomfort-resolved: suspicious that this is a combination of inactivity and fluid overload. monitor for any changes. # Diarrhea: resolved; on anti-diarrheals. C-Diff negative. # Infectious prophylaxis: - PCP: no ___- angioedema rxn initate atovaquone D+21 - HSV/VZV: acyclovir -Bacterial: see tx ABX * Of note, patient has angioedema and urticaria rxn to sulfa/cephalosporin/PCN ABX, if spikes okay to use aztreonam/vanc/FQ. Prophylaxes: # Access: L CVC--avoid CHG # FEN: regular low bacteria diet # Pain control: oxy prn # Code status: full
summary: You were admitted to receive an autologous stem cell transplant. You developed nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea secondary to the chemotherapy. You also developed a fever when your counts were low and developed pneumonia. You were placed on antibiotics which were discontinued ___. You will likely continue to need anti-nausea medication at home for some time. Please use either ativan or compazine prior to eating or taking meds if you find this helps you. We gave you a steroid and an antinausea medication called Emend on ___. You will take one final dose of Emend at home. Please check your temperature twice daily at home, and call if it goes over 100.4. Please drink 2liters of fluid at home (the equivalent of 3 20oz bottles) daily. Eating will likely take a while to come back, so eat things that are bland. Your symptoms have improved and your appetite and liquid intake has greatly improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old obese man with a history of CAD s/p CABG, COPD on 3L O2 at home ___ FEV1 0.44, 13% predicted), OSA, HFpEF (LVEF=50%), congenital bicuspid aortic valve s/p AVR ___ valve), HTN, insulin dependent diabetes who presented with acute on chronic shortness of breath and left-sided chest pain. #Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: Patient was 257lbs on admission. He reported gaining 12 pounds in the previous two weeks. He received BID IV Lasix 40mg with a net negative goal of ___. TTE demonstrated LVEF 50% with well seated aortic valve bioprothesis with increased gradient and mild aortic regurgitation. Mild symmetric LVH with mild global hypokinesis and mild-moderate MR. ___ discussed with outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___, who agreed with plan to transition to PO Torsemide 30mg BID upon discharge. His discharge weight was 247.5lbs. Discharge creatinine 0.8. We continued his metoprolol succinate on discharge. #COPD: Patient has a history of COPD on 3L of oxygen at home. He was recently hospitalized in ___ for COPD exacerbation and reported increased cough and sputum production in the past week. He completed a 5-day course of azithromycin and prednisone 80mg, and had standing duonebs. Upon discharge, patient returned to home prednisone 35mg with plans to continue taper. Other home inhalers were continued (he was treated with standing albuterol/ipratropium). #Facial numbness/blurry vision: The patient reported one day of blurry vision and right-sided facial numbness and the feeling that his face was drooping. There were no focal neurologic deficits. We consulted the Neurology Team for concern of TIA vs. stroke. He had a CTA head and neck and a non-contrast MRI head which showed no signs of infarction or hemorrhage. Of note, there was some difficulty obtaining an MRI of his head given concern that his porcine valve had a metal component. Records from ___ indicate that he has a ___ valve. Patient was provided with copies of these records. He remains on prophylaxis with antiplatelet and lipid lowering agents. #Asymmetric leg swelling: Patient was noted to have 3+ pitting edema on exam with his left leg larger than his right leg. He had Doppler of his left lower extremity which showed no signs of a thrombus. ============== CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #Diabetes: Continued home Glargine 40U. #CAD: Continued home aspirin and statin. #GERD: Continued home omeprazole. #Depression: We continued his home citalopram #MVI: Continued his home vitamin D, calcium carbonate ===================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== # Discharge weight: 247 lbs. # Discharge Cr: 0.8 # Diastolic heart failure. Please check daily weights; notify MD if >3 lbs increase in 24 hours. # Medication changes. Switched furosemide to torsemide 30 mg BID. Please follow up electrolytes at next visit. # Blurry vision. Advised to follow up with optometry for refraction check. # CODE: Full code (confirmed) # CONTACT: ___ (Sister) Phone number: ___ Cell phone: ___
summary: You were admitted for increased shortness of breath, chest pain, and blurry vision. We believe that your increased shortness of breath was related to a COPD and CHF exacerbation. To address your COPD exacerbation, you completed a 5-day course of prednisone 80mg and azithromycin. For your CHF exacerbation, you were given IV Lasix and eventually transitioned to a new oral medication called torsemide. You also had lower extremity swelling, with the left leg noted to be larger than the right leg. You had an ultrasound of your left leg which showed no blood clots. You also reported sharp, left-sided chest pain. Your EKG was unchanged from previously and your labs were reassuring against a heart attack. You chest pain improved during your hospitalization. You also mentioned having blurry vision and right-sided facial numbness and feeling of face drooping. You were seen by the Neurology Team. You had a CT and MRI of your head which showed no signs of an acute stroke.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history of CAD s/p CABG, complete heart block s/p pacemaker placement, HTN, HLD, and DM who presented to the ED after a fall and was found to be significantly anemic. . # Fall: By history seems to be syncope. The DDx for syncope includes arrhythmia, hypovolemia, and anemia. Given his new anemia, this seems to be a significant contributor, but other factors may have been involved. Peripheral neuropathy may be a contributing factor to his fall as well. Monitored on telemetry. Fall precautions in place. ___ evaluation has been completed; patient will further receive ___ evaluation at home. Treated anemia as below. Decrease gabapentin to 200mg PO HS (see below). ECHO to evaluate valvular disease and structural heart disease. ECHO suggests patient's heart is at least at baseline and may be stronger than from last ECHO. . # Anemia: A relatively new microcytic anemia with guaiac positive stool suggests a subacute or chronic GI bleed, but bleeding from other sites could also be involved. Past SPEP showed monoclonal gammopathy of unknown significance, which may have progressed to MM and be a source of his anemia. Hypothyroidism can cause a microcytic anemia. Chronic renal insufficiency is likely contributing as well. It seems unlikely that autoimmune hemolytic process is active here given his low LDH. Iron studies suggested iron-deficiency. B12 and folic acid within normal limits. SPEP shows immununoglobulin within normal limits. UPEP demonstrated no ___ proteins. TSH within normal limitis. DAT (Coombs) pending Haptoglobin is within normal limits.Reticulocytes within normal limits. . The patient was started on omeprazole 20mg PO BID. Transfused 2 unit of PRBCs and H/H rose appropriately. Started on iron 325mg PO daily with vitamin c 500mg PO daily to increase iron bioavailability on a PPI. Started on folic acid 1mg PO daily and vitamin B12 250mcg PO daily. Stopped clopidogrel and decrease ASA to 81mg PO daily (see below) Switched gabapentin to 200mg PO HS (see below). GI has been consulted and found ulcers in stomach and duodenum; omeprazole dose doubled again. . # PUD, gastritis, duodenitis Per GI recommendation, omeprazole dose has been doubled to 40 mg. Ulcers show no current signs of bleeding; may have been recently bleeding. Patient may follow up as outpatient with GI. . # CAD: Distant history of CABG. Stopped clopidogrel 75mg PO daily as it had unclear indications in this patient and was likely contributing to his ongoing bleeding. Restarted due to concerns for his carotid stents. Decreased ASA to 81mg PO daily but did not stop this medication as recent data suggests that even in the setting of an active GIB, holding all antiplatlet therapy increases cardiac morbidity out of proportion to bleeding morbidity. Continued home atorvastatin 80mg PO HS. Continued home lisinopril 5mg PO daily. . # Neuropathy: It is possible that gabapentin is contributing to his fall risk. However, he has neuropathic tingling especially at night. Switched gabapentin to 200mg PO HS. . # Chronic renal insufficiency: Baseline creatinine fluctuates between 1.5 and 1.8mg/dL. His creatinine is in that range on admission. Renally dosed all medication. Avoided nephrotoxins. SPEP, UPEP within normal limits. Added IV fluids today. Creatinine unchanged. Creatinine is stable. . # Diabetes: Would return to metformin once creatinine drops below 1.5. . # Glaucoma: Continued home bimatoprost 0.03% 1 drip ___ HS. Continued home brimonidine 0.15% 1 drop ___ TID. Continued home dorzolamide / timolol 2% / 0.5% 1 drop ___ HS . # Depression: Continued home escitalopram 5mg PO daily. . # Osteoporosis: - Continued Calcium / vitamin D 600mg / 400units PO daily.
summary: You came into the hospital after a fall. We believe that your fall was caused by anemia, which is a blood condition that can make you weak and tired. We transfused two units of red blood cells to keep your blood levels up. We also ran a series of tests to determine what was causing your anemia. The lab tests showed that it was not likely because of problems with your bones making blood. It is much more likely that the problem is that you are bleeding in your digestive tract. Because we suspected this bleeding, we had GI specialists come visit you, and they decided to take a look at your espohagus and stomach with a camera. They found that you had some ulcers in your stomach. The ulcers were not bleeding at the time, but there was evidence that they had been bleeding recently. Because of your anemia, we made a few changes to your medications. Mostly we added some vitamins and iron that will help your blood recover. We also reduced the dose of your aspirin and increased the dose of a medicine to reduce stomach acid because of the ulcers in your stomach. Because your blood pressure was high during your last day, we increased the dose of your blood pressure medication also. START taking 40 mg omeprazole (instead of 20 mg) twice daily. START taking 81 mg (a baby aspirin, instead of full strength) aspirin once daily. START taking 1 mg folic acid once daily. START taking 250 mcg B12 vitamin (cyanocobalamin) once daily. START taking 500 mg ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) once daily. The Vitain C helps you absorb iron. Take the vitamin C at the same time as your iron. START taking 300 mg ferrous sulfate (iron) once daily with the Vitamin C. START taking lisinopril 10 mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old man on Coumadin for DVT prophylaxis due to a DVT diagnosed in ___, C. difficile colitis one month prior to admission, external hemorrhoids, and diverticulosis on colonoscopy one year before admission, who was admitted with 6 days of BRPRB likely due to a lower GI bleed from diverticulosis, external hemorrhoids, an angiodysplasia, or persistent C difficile colitis. The patient had ___ loose stools the day before admission with maroon blood and clots accompanying the stool, on the outside of the stool. He had accompanying perspiration, mild chills, and ___ pressure in his LLQ. He never had fevers. In the ED the patient had gross red blood on rectal exam, and an anoscope exam showed a non-bleeding external hemorrhoid and an area of inflamed anal mucosa in the posterior wall of the rectum. He was afebrile, without leukocytosis, and with a supratheraputic INR of 3.8. His Coumadin was held. 1. BRBPR with loose stools. On the day of admission, he had one normal non-bloody stool. The morning after being admitted, he had a stool in the morning that had minimal blood on the outside of the stool. The sweating and mild chills he had in the 5 days before admission had significantly improved. Serial hematocrits showed a stable, normal HCT in the ___. He remained afebrile, and the sensation of pressure in his LLQ was gone. It was thought that his BRBPR was likely due to either hemorrhoidal or diverticular bleeding. The morning after admission he continued to be without a leukocytosis and with a supratheraputic INR of 3.9. A colonoscopy was scheduled for ___, and his Coumadin was arranged to be held until that date. He was scheduled to see his PCP for ___ follow up appointment and laboratory tests on ___ to confirm his BRBPR and loose stools continued to improve. He will also follow-up on the results of his repeat clostridium difficile testing done during this hospitalization to rule out the possibility of persistent infection. He may require repeat blood testing of INR on ___ if it remains >2 on ___, ___. GI recommends an INR of less than two in order to complete the colonoscopy; the patient and PCP are aware of this. 2. Depression, ADD, anxiety. On the night of admission the patient said he was at his baseline level of depression and was without suicidal ideation or homicidal ideation. He said he felt hopeless, anhedonic, spending increased amounts of time sleeping, and felt fatigued and tired. His Fluoxetine dose was increased recently from 40 to 60 mg, yet he has never experienced significant alleviation of his depression with fluoxetine or any other antidepressant (of which he has tried many). He was discharged on his outpatient dose of fluoxetine. He was also discharged on his outpatient dose of Dextroamphetamine 40 mg PO daily, and Clonazepam 0.5 mg PO BID PRN for anxiety and insomnia. It was recommended that he continue to work with his psychopharmacologist and psychotherapist with close follow-up as an outpatient. The patient was deemed medically stable and fit for discharge on ___ with close follow-up with his PCP and scheduled colonoscopy as outpatient to further evaluate source of rectal bleeding.
summary: you presented to the hospital because of bleeding from the rectum that you noticed became increasingly worse in the six days prior to coming to the hospital. Given that you are on coumadin, which is a blood thinner, you were admitted for further evaluation and management of this lower gastrointestinal bleed. Given that you have a history of diverticular disease and hemorrhoids, it is likely that the source of your bleed is either of these. However, other etiologies such as inflammation or polyps should be ruled out, and thus you will be scheduled for an outpatient colonoscopy as outlined below. During this admission, we closely monitored your hematocrit, which is a blood level that can show us how acute your bleed is. Your hematocrit was noted to be stable throughout this admission, as were your blood pressure and other vital signs. Given your stable presentation, and elevated INR of 3.8 from recent coumadin use, it was decided to continue to hold your coumadin and complete the colonoscopy on an outpatient basis. Prior to your colonoscopy, you will need to have a repeat complete blood count and INR checked. Your INR should be <2 in order to have this procedure. Please do not take your coumadin until after you have had the colonoscopy, and only then as recommended by Dr. ___. You will need to complete a bowel preparation with magnesium citrate the night before, and have nothing to eat except your medications after midnight the evening before the procedure is scheduled. In addition, you should only have a clear liquid diet as of 8 ___ on the day prior to the colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ man with a history of anxiety, depression, CAD s/p MI and DES to distal RCA, PVD with caudication who presents with leg swelling, leg pain and chest pain. #Active Issues: # CORONARIES: Pt has a hx of MI in ___ (trop to 0.3, flat CKMB), with most recent cath showing LAD 80% ostial D1 lesions and diffuse RCA dissease with mid-vessel 50% lesion but distal RCA ___. Pt also has known history of extensive PAD. Symptoms on presentation were consistent with unstable angina, increasing in frequency, severity, happening at rest. In the differential was PE, but patient had a Well's score of 0 and a TIMI score of 3. He was initially treated with Nitro and Morphine 2mg IV for the mild chest pain he still had on arrival to the floor. Heparin gtt was not started for concern of patient's reported allergies to Heparin, Integrillin. Patient himself was unaware of these allergies, which per the patient's chart stated they cause thrombocytopenia. He had negative Heparin dependent antibodies during a previous admission and has never had an episode of HIT. The patient's pain was well controlled with nitro and morphine and he went for cardiac catheterization on ___. He was found to have only non obstructive chronic CAD and no intervention was performed. The distal RCA stent was patent. Throughout admission patient was maintained on his home medication regimen of Metoprolol 12.5mg BID, Lisinopril 2.5mg daily, ASA 325mg daily, Plavix 75mg daily. He was started on Atorvastatin 80mg daily and Rosuvastatin 40mg daily was discontinued. This admission patient was started on Imdur 30mg daily which he tolerated well. # PVD: s/p fem-fem bypass Right to Left in ___ with follow up studies in ___ showing good blood flow. Patient had good pulses on exam bilaterally, strongest at dorsalis pedis but still palpable posterior tibialis. No parasthesias, numbness in legs, no edema on exam. His leg pain was ___ on admission which decreased to ___ on day of discharge. He had arterial noninvasives which were normal with triphasic flow pattern throughout. Vascular surgery was consulted for potential intervention but as patient was found to have good pulses and normal art ext noninvasives, intervention was not needed at this time. He has follow up appointment to vascular surgery clinic in a month with pre-clinic dupplex doppler of his fem-fem graft. He will continue on ASA 325mg, Plavix 75mg and Cilostazol. Chronic Issues: # HTN: continued Metoprolol and Lisinopril as above. Added Imdur this admission. Blood pressure was well controlled throughout admission. # HLD: labs this admission showed elevated LDL to 133, he was put on Atorvastatin 80mg while inpatient, and discharged on this dose. Patient continued Niacin SR 1500mg daily. # DM: HbA1c was 7.1% this admission. Glipizide and Metformin were held in house and patient was given sliding scale insulin. Can resume home medications on discharge. # Anxiety/Depression, Mood disorder: Patient continued all of his home medications at this time: Cont klonopin 2mg po tid, Cont Seroquel 600mg po HS and 200mg at noon, Cont Venlafaxine ER 75mg po daily, Cont valoproate 1g bid for mood stabilization, Cont folate/pyridoxine # History of PUD - Cont Ranitidine and Omeprazole Transitional issues: # CODE: FULL CODE (confirmed) # EMERGENCY CONTACT: Stepdaughter ___ ___ # Will need to follow up with Dr. ___ ___ # Follow up with vascular surgery for further evaluation of resting leg pain.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after having worsening chest and leg pain over the last two months. Your symptoms were concerning for worsening of your coronary artery disease and you were taken for cardiac catheterization to evaluate your coronary arteries. No intervention was done during this procedure because your arteries did not look like they had enough blockage to cause problems with the heart. You additionally had imaging studies done of your legs to assess for worsening of your peripheral vascualar disease. The blood flow to your legs was reviewed by the vascular surgeons and they did not think any surgery was indicated. They would like to see you in clinic in one month to re-evaluate your bypass graft.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ yo F with hx of breast ca s/p radiation ___, hypertension, depression and utero-vaginal prolapse s/p pessary placement admitted for pyelonephritis. Hospital course by problem: 1. Pyelo - pt's CVAT, nausea/vomiting, fever, leukocytosis with L shift and positive UA were highly suggestive of pyelo. However, urine cx showed contamination and grew mixed flora x 2. Pt was treated with ciprofloxacin IV initially due to inability to tolerate POs. She quickly defervesced. She was transitioned to PO cipro late in the day of admission and tolerated a full diet. She was counseled to continue treatment until ___. GYN was curbside consulted on the utility of removing the pessary; they said it was not necessary unless pessary apeared to be obstructing (which team did not believe was happening). 2. Nephrolithiasis - Noted to have persistent WBC, followed by ARF and hematuria. Given these findings, ultrasound then CT was performed. Urology was consulted and placed a ureteral stent. The day after procedure, the pt had persistant hematuria and flank pain and was examined by urology who said sx were WNL for post-procedure. Pt will follow-up with urology in ___ wks for uretoscopy and laser lithotripsy. Pt will continue cipro for 5 d post op. 3. Acute Renal Failure - on day 2 of admission, Cr bumped from 0.7 to 1.2. Renal US was obtained which showed renal cysts and ?obstruction. CT abdomen was done which showed a 6 mm stone at the UP junction. Renal lytes were consistent with prerenal cause. ARF resolved on following day. 4. hyperglycemia - pt has no known hx of diabetes, but random glucose was found to be elevated. HbA1c was checked and was 5.4. 5. hypertension - bp was elevated in ED to 200s systolic, but came down after treatment with morphine and was presumed related to pain. Pt also developed ha in ED, but pt had no neuro sx and ha resolved. Home atenolol was continued. 6. wt loss - per pt, likely due to depression and poor appetite at home. However, TSH has never been checked and pt has never had colonoscopy. TSH was checked and was WNL. Pt was recommended to have colonoscopy as outpt. SPEP and UPEP were also checked, which were pending at time of d/c. 7. Low O2 sat - on admission to the floor, pt was found to have O2 sat of 90% on RA. Pt was asx. CXR was completed which showed mild pulmonary congestion and small bilateral pleural effusions. O2 sats improved and pt did not receive diuresis. 8. depression - pt continued home citalopram 9. osteopenia - pt continued home Vit D and was started on supplemental Ca ___. neurodermatitis - pt continued sarna topical cream 11. hx of breast ca - no current issues. ___ ___ nl. 12. lytic lesions in pelvis - On CT pelvis, small lytic lesions seen that could be associated with multiple myeloma, although given breast ca hx, recurrence is also in the differential. SPEP and UPEP were checked and pending at time of discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for an infection in your urinary tract that extended up to your kidney along with kidney stones. While you were in the hospital, several tests were done. An exam of your urine showed signs of infection. An ultrasound of your kidneys showed cysts and question of obstruction. A CT of your abdomen showed a kidney stone that was obstructing your kidney. This was treated with a stent placement by urology. Your white blood cell count was elevated which is a sign of infection. Finally, a chest x-ray was done while you were in the hospital. It showed mild pulmonary congestion. Your home medicines were continued while you were in the hospital. In addition, you were started on antibiotics which should be continued until ___. In addition, you were started on calcium supplement which along with Vitamin D helps to keep your bones strong and prevent osteoporosis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with EtOH cirrhosis (Child's B, MELD 11, being evaluated for transplant), h/o varices (1 cord Grade 1 in ___, encephalopathy, and diuretic controlled ascites, as well as recurrent abdominal pain who presents with acute episode of abdominal pain now resolved with dysuria, CVA tenderness, and pyuria concerning for pyelonephritis. #Pyelopnephritis Patiet presented with pyuria on UA, burning with urination, fever prior to admission, positive CVA tenderness with past history of VRE UTI's concerning for pyelonephritis. Patient was started on IV ceftriaxone and narrowed to Cipro for 7 day course to end ___ with instructions that someone from medical team will call if needs to change antibiotics. #Abdominal pain: Patient with prior history of chronic abdominal pain presenting with new acute pain in the midepigastrium. Per outside records patient had known history of duodenal ulcers on recent EGD that may have been etiology of her pain. She has known diverticulosis but did not have clear clinical signs of diverticulosis. In addition CT scan per outside records also showed T10 lesion that was also thought to potentially be contributing to her symptoms, she was instructed to follow up with PCP for further work up and treatment. She was discharged with 3 days of oxycodone (12 pills) with expectation that ulcer pain will improve now that PPI dose increased. #Asthma with cough Patient noted cough over the week prior to admission in setting of known asthma. Influenza was ruled out. CXR also obtained that did not show focal process. CHRONIC STABLE ISSUES ## CIRRHOSIS: EtOH cirrhosis (Child's B, MELD 11, being evaluated for transplant), h/o varices (1 cord Grade 1 in ___, encephalopathy, and diuretic controlled ascites. Patient did not have evidence of encephalopathy and rifaxamin 550 mg BID and lactulose 30 mg TID continued. Lasix and spironolactone held initially in setting of infection. ## HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY: Continued lactulose & rifaximin ## GIB/VARICES: Grade 1 only on EGD ___ - Child's B, no history of bleeding so not on nadolol ## ASCITES: - diuretic controlled - hold spironolactone and furosemide in setting of possible pyelopephritis ## NUTRITION: - Low sodium diet - 2L fluid restrict - continue folate #Depression: - continue risperidone, citalopram # Constipation: - continue senna, docusate, lactulose, miralax TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =============== -f/u duodenal ulcer biopsies from outside hospital -continue BID PPI, repeat endoscopy in 1.5 months -f/u T10 lesion -urine cultures pending
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for abdominal pain and you had burning with urination, flank pain, and evidence of infection in your urine and were treated for kidney infection. We believe you are on effective antibiotics, but if your final urine cultures and require a different antibiotic, someone from our team will call you. We also obtained outside records that showed that you have known duodenal ulcers. You were already on omeprazole 40 mg daily but we recommend that you increase this to 40 mg twice daily, and please continue to drink ___ glasses of fluid daily (but not more than 2 liters, or the size of a large soda bottle). Please see Dr. ___ again in 1.5 months, as he plans to repeat endocscopy. We also noted that on your CT scan from another hospital showed a nodule about the size of a small marble near your spine. We recommend you discuss this with your primary care doctor, in case it might be causing nerve pain in that area because there is a chance you could benefit from injections for pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of spinal osteomyelitis, depression, hypertension, who is presenting following an MRI that is concerning for C1 osteomyelitis vs noninfectious degenerative cause. =================================== ACUTE MEDICAL ISSUES ADDRESSED =================================== # Neck pain # Degenerative changes of cervical spine Patient with MRI at outside hospital concerning for osteomyelitis, though with motion artifact. She had a repeat MRI in the ED, which was less concerning for infection, but also was degraded by motion artifact. She was treated with antibiotics in the emergency department, but these were stopped given no clinical signs of osteo, with a normal white count and CRP. A repeat MRI was done with sedation. This showed degenerative changes of the C1-C2 spine, with no evidence of osteomyelitis. It also showed a likely syrinx formation from C2 to C7-T1. The ortho spine team reviewed the films, and felt that there is no surgical indication at this time. They will have her follow up in clinic, and also plan to discuss the large syrinx with neurosurgery to see if any further intervention would be needed in the outpatient setting. =================================== CHRONIC MEDICAL ISSUES ADDRESSED =================================== # Seizure disorder - continued home keppra # Hypertension - continued home metoprolol, amlodipine # Depression - continued home citalopram # Restless leg syndrome - continued home tizanidine # GERD - continued home omeprazole =================================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================================== [] Patient should be seen in ortho spine clinic by Dr. ___ in ___ weeks following discharge. [] Ortho spine to discuss syrinx with neurosurgery at multidisciplinary meeting [] Patient discharged with 8 tabs of oxycodone 5mg for pain control. Advised to not drive while taking this medication. Can discuss if further pain medication is needed at primary care appointment # CONTACT: ___ ___ # CODE STATUS: Full (presumed)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after an MRI showed a possible infection in your spine. We were having trouble getting a good picture of this, so we sedated you in order to get the best picture possible. This picture showed no infection, which is good news. However, it did show some damage to the bone. We spoke to the spine surgeons, and they did not think that you needed to have an operation. However, they would like to keep seeing you in spine clinic. It is very important that you follow up with them in clinic. We would also like for you to see your primary care doctor this week. Please speak with them about working to manage your pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with CHF (LVEF unknown), atrial fibrillation, hypertension, back pain, osteoarthritis, cataracts, hearing loss, and memory loss who presented with two weeks of progressive dyspnea and a 15 lb weight gain consistent with a CHF exacerbation. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Acute on Chronic Diastolic CHF: He has a history of CHF, RV predominant, likely secondary to left-sided diastolic CHF. At presentation he had bilateral pleural effusion, distended neck veins, and significant pedal, scrotal, and penile edema consistent with biventricular failure. Echo showed preserved LVEF, markedly dilated right ventricle with severe pulmonary hypertension and moderate-to-severe tricuspid regurgitation, marked ventricular interaction secondary to severe right ventricular pressure and volume overload, and prominent pleural effusions and ascites. ___ cardiology was consulted. He was diuresed with Lasix IV and then switch to high PO regimen for discharge. After being stable on PO for 24 hours he was discharged to rehab for continued care. Weights obtained were not consistent but based on UOP and clinically he was markedly improved. He will follow up with ___ cardiology. # Atrial Fibrillation, Bradycardia: No longer anticoagulated with warfarin out of concern for falls at home (confirmed with HCP and home health aide). He was monitored on telemetry, which showed irregularly irregular bradycardia, at times to the ___, though asymptomatic wit stable blood pressures, with frequent PVCs and intermittent, brief runs of asymptomatic NSVT. During his episodes of bradycardia he was completely asymptomatic when awake and while sleeping. His electrolytes were monitored and replaced as needed. His home ASA was continued. # Concerns for falls at home: He worked with ___ during this admission, who recommended it was safer that he go to rehab for further recovery and ___ after discharge. He has a ___ for when he goes home. Per ___, he should ambulate with a walker. # Change of code status: The patient decided to become full code during this admission. He cited as his reasons the desire to help those who would be treating him learn from the experience. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Depression / Dementia: His mood was bright during this admission and he remained well-oriented and communicative during this admission. His home sertraline and risperidone were continued. # Benign Hypertension: Pressures were stable during diuresis. He was continued on his home Losartan. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - He should follow-up with Atris cardiology as an outpatient. ## Contact information: Outpt case manager, ___ (___) Phone ___, ___ HCP and power of attorney ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the ___ because of increasing shortness of breath and leg swelling. We determined that you have an exacerbation of congestive heart failure (a condition in which fluid backs up from the heart), resulting in fluid in the lungs as well as, quite prominently, in your legs. We treated you with intravenous diuretics (like your water pill) with close monitoring of your electrolytes. Your swelling diminished markedly and you are ready to be discharged. You have been switched to a new oral lasix regimen (increased dose) that you can take at home. Before going home, though, we have discharged you to a rehab facility where you can work with physical therapists to regain your strength and balance. Please review your medication list closely.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old woman with HTN, HLD, depression, hypothyroidism who presents with 3 episodes of left hand and face tingling, with mild dysarthria, on head CT found with a small right frontal convexal SAH. Three episodes prior to admission-- ___ spell: 2 weeks ago with 30 seconds of slurred speech and LUE circumferential tingling, ___ spell: day PTA with tingling in the fingers/hand 1 min, ___: more pronounced slurred speech and hand tingling. No episodes were associated with headache and no history of recent trauma. Of note, a few years of minor memory issues-- forgetting names, misplacing objects, etc. Throughout her hospital course her neurological exam remained normal, however she continued to have brief episode similar to the ones that brought her in. The MRI appeared consistent with convexal SAH but the etiology was not known. Our initial CTA showed prominence of R frontal vasculature suggestive of possible AVM and Neurosurgery took her for cerebral angiogram on ___ which was negative. We suspect that this bleed may be related to early amyloid angiopathy, given her age and cognitive complaints, although the MRI GRE sequence did not show diffuse GRE signal. During this hospitalization we stopped ASA (plan to hold indefinitely given weak indication for ASA). We are continuing home statin, close BP control with goal <140 systolic. The question of events related to possible seizure was addressed throughout this admission. Dr. ___ was the admitting attending, felt that the symptoms were most likely related to cortical spreading depression (migrainous phenomenom) rather than seizure and EEG/AEDs were deferred. On day of discharge the service was taken over by Dr. ___ preference was to treat empirically, especially if events persist. She will follow up with Dr. ___ call his office to start keppra if bothersome events persist. At discharge she had refused AEDs. Incidentally throughout this hospitalization she had asymptomatic SVT to 140s mostly during sleep. She was given a home holter monitor and discussed with primary care doctor who will follow up. Transition Issues - SBP <140 - holding ASA indefinitely given bleed - if persistent events will call Dr. ___ initiation of AED (low dose keppra preferred) - Holter monitor for SVT and PCP follow up ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 1 SPRY NU DAILY:PRN congestion 2. Pravastatin 5 mg PO QPM 3. Sertraline 50 mg PO DAILY 4. Acyclovir 400 mg PO 5X/DAY FOR 5 DAYS AS NEEDED 5. famciclovir 125 mg oral BID:PRN rash 6. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (250/50) 1 INH IH BID 7. Levothyroxine Sodium 100 mcg PO DAILY 8. Albuterol Sulfate (Extended Release) 90 mcg PO Q6H:PRN sob/cough 9. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 10. Ca ___ bor 250 mg calcium -500 unit oral Other 11. Vitamin D 400 UNIT PO DAILY 12. Estrogens Conjugated use a small amt on tip of finger directed once per week and when exchange of pessary gm VG ASDIR 13. lisinopril-hydrochlorothiazide ___ mg oral DAILY 14. ___ (cranberry extract) 400 mg oral DAILY 15. coenzyme Q10 100 mg oral DAILY 16. cranberry 600 mg oral DAILY 17. Fish Oil 1200mg-144mg-216mg oral BID 18. Glucosamine-Chondroitin Complex ___ mg oral DAILY 19. Multivitamins W/minerals 1 TAB PO DAILY 20. Vitamin B Complex w/C 1 TAB PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the Neurology Service with episodes of left sided paresthesias. On CT scan you were found to have a small bleed that likely caused your symptoms. On MRI we found that you had a small, stable hemorrhage called a subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). This kind of blood can sometimes cause brief episodes of numbness/tingling like the kind you report. We do not think these were seizures, and we did not give you medications to control them. You may continue to have intermittent spells like the one that brought you to the hospital, but we do not think these are dangerous and are likely to improve as your brain heals from this injury.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o syncope x~6, the last of which was in ___ now s/p unwitnessed syncopal episode. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Syncope: Given how often urination is involved, increased vagal tone suspected. Question as to whether this causes pure vasovagal, leads to sinus pauses, or whether this is truly neurocardiogenic. Given his possible orthopnea and PND, there is a question of cardiac involvement. The episodes do not sound neurologic (CVA, epileptic), nor hypoglycemic. Though he has mechanical problems and orthostasis, these are not at all historically compatible with the episodes the patient describes. Notably his tox was negative and chemistries nl in the ED. He does state that sometimes he wakes up immediately after a fall but feels so fatigued that he falls back asleep on the floor. None of his medications are strongly anticholinergic (notably, he has not been taking his diphenhydramine or vitamins recently due to financial difficulties). The patient's telemetry was unrevealing and EKG showed only nonspecific anterolateral T wave changes. After fluid resuscitation for ___ (below), the patient's heart rate was noted to increase from 100 to 140-160 on standing by his nurse. This suggested an orthostatic component that is likely representative of an autonomic neuropathy as he was volume replete. This is certainly consistent with his peripheral neuropathy secondary to alcohol. His TSH and zinc were normal. Vitamin B studies had yet to return at the time of discharge. It was thought that the patient's syncope was multifactorial and could be attributed to components of both micturition syncope and autonomic neuropathy-mediated orthostasis. . # ___: Cr 1.3 from baseline ~1. Likely pre-renal given pt's history of orthostatic episodes. The patient was volume resuscitated and his creatinine resolved to its baseline. . # Back pain: chronic problem with no acute progression in sensory deficits or weakness. No incontinence, making acute SC processes unlikely. He was treated with acetaminophen and opiates. . # Headache: the patient received analgesia as above with speedy resolution of his headache. . # Depressed mood: The patient denied suicidal ideation. Social work was not able to see him over the weekend and they were similarly occupied while he remained inpatient on the day of his discharge. . INACTIVE ISSUES: none . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Syncope: The patient was discharged with moderate-pressure compression stockings in an effort to decrease the orthostatic component of his syncope. We recommend tilt table testing to parse out whether there is a definite vasovagal component to his episodes that might be successfully treated with low-dose propanolol. Although he had not been taking his Benadryl, this was formally discontinued. . # Depression: the patient was given a social work phone number to call so that he can be referred to appropriate psychiatric services.
summary: As you recall, you were admitted after you were found on the floor having sustained a syncopal episode. It was thought that your episode was related to the need to urinate, as have many of your past episodes. Initial heart testing was unremarkable and your episodes did not seem consistent with low blood pressure on standing, seizure, stroke, or simply a mechanical fall. Your hospital course was remarkable for an episode of low blood pressure on standing, but you did not lose consciousness. For the near future, you should urinate sitting down. You should take your time getting up from a sitting or lying position, to let your body equilibrate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old with a history of COPD and bronchiolitis obliterans in addition to prior episodes of septic arthritis of her L ankle who presents with L ankle pain and shortness of breath. # Dyspnea: likely component of acute COPD exacerbation in setting of baseline bronchiectasis and bronchiolitis. Green sputum production concerning for pneumonia, specifically with pseudomonas given that patient has structural lung disease. However, patient does not have leukocytosis or evidence of pneumonia on CXR. Patient was given prednisone 60mg for presumed COPD exacerbation along with increasing azithromycin to 500 daily x 5 days and then may return back to her prior doses of those medications. Patient received standing albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers as well as her home maintenance medications. -cont spiriva -cont advair 500/50 -Prednisone 60mg PO daily ___ (chose ___ given patient on longstanding steroids). -Continue Bactrim 3x/week -Increase azithromycin to daily for 5 days, then back to 3 days/week (___) # Ankle swelling: unlikely to be septic joint, however was aspirated by orthopedics,and gram stain shows no organism, culture no growth. Most likely related to RA flare. Elected not to cover with antibiotics at this time given exam not consistent with septic joint, no sign of sepsis. That being said, patient on steroids which minimize symptoms of infection. CT ___ was non specific and could indicate osteo, however more likely related to prior destruction of the joint. Bone biopsy would likely be only way to definitively rule out osteomyelitis, however this appears to be overly aggressive for something that has low pre-test probability. This was discussed with Dr. ___ ___, outapatient rheumatologist who stated he would have her see infectious disease as an outpatient. #Rheumatoid Arthritis: severe. patient on hydroxychloriquine as outpatient as well as chronic steroids. Also with Ibuprofen. Ankle likely represents RA flare. No indication for acute change at this time. -cont hydroxychloroquine -cont prednisone as noted above -cont ibuprofen # GERD: complicated by ___ esophagus, continued omeprazole 20 mg BID (especially given prednisone use) # anxiety/depression: continued fluoxetine, trazodone, PRN lorazepam. # HCM: - continued home ASA, folic acid. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient should follow up with PCP and pulmonologist regarding COPD exacerbations and indications for daily azithromycin versus her current regimen -Patient should follow up with her rheumatologist and infectious disease regarding her ankle
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of difficulty breathing and pain in your ankle. While you were here you were thought to have an exacerbation of your COPD (for which you received prednisone and azithromycin) and your ankle does not appear to be infected. Please take the prednisone 60mg and azithromycin daily for 2 more days (last day ___. At that time you can change your prednisone to 40mg per day for 2 days, then 20mg for 2 days, then back to your home 15 mg. Your ankle was tapped for fluid but not found to have any evidence of infection. A Ct scan was done, which was non specific given that your joint already had a lot of damage.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with PMH notable for L knee replacement in ___, recent admission for cellulitis in end of ___ now direct admit for persistent cellulitis, also C diff pos. Active issues: # Cellulitis: Initialy on 3 weeks of oral antibiotics (keflex/bactrim). MRI neg for osteo. Ultrasound showed no abscess/pocket for drainage. Per ID, patient was started on PO cipro and IV vancomycin. Blood cultures negative (some pending at time of discharge). Patient to follow-up with infectious disease who may adjust planned 2 week course of antibiotics. # C.diff colitis: Found to be C.diff positive in the setting of 3 weeks of antibiotics. Patient started on Flagyl 500mg PO q8hrs, to be continued for 2 weeks after stopping the Cipro/Flagyl as above. Chronic issues: # Depression/anxiety: Continued home bupropion and ativan # Left shoulder pain: Patient with known rotator cuff tear. Followed by orthopedics as an outpt and s/p steroid injection ___. Tylenol PRN. Transitional issues: -CODE: Full (confirmed) -EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (husband) ___ -Needs follow-up with PCP and ID to assess for resolution of this resistant cellulitis
summary: You were admitted with left foot cellulitis. We were concerned that ___ could have osteomyelitis (an infection of the bone). An MRI scan was performed which showed no osteomyelitis. We had the infectious disease specialists see ___ in the hospital. They recommended a combination of an IV antibiotic called vancomycin and an antibiotic by mouth called ciprofloxacin. We started ___ on these medications in the hospital. You were also found to have an infection in the bowel called C. difficile colitis (sometimes just called "C. diff"). We started ___ on an antibiotic by mouth called flagyl to treat this infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ y/o patient with a complicated medical history significant for alcohol abuse, cirrhosis, hepatitis C, diabetes, HTN, CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___ (refused cath at the time) who initially presented at ___ for chest pain and shortness of breath and was found to have a NSTEMI with troponin peak 2.0 and a platelet count of 50,000. He was transferred to ___ for evaluation for possible platelets transfusion prior to catheterization and possible PCI. He underwent catheterization on ___ and received two BMS in proximal and mid RCA lesions. . #NSTEMI: Patient with a history of CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___ presented at OSH for chest pain and shortness of breath. He was found to have troponin elevations with a peak at 2.___epression in V5. His pain was relieved with morphine and he was started on a heparin drip. As his CXR showed mild pulmonary edema, he receives 20 mg lasix IV. His echo showed normal LV wall motion with an EF=65% and normal RV function. At our hospital, we continued his heparin drip, started him on pravastatin 10 mg and continued his home medication. Patient was catheterized on ___. For contrast dye allergy he received three doses of Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV Q4h and benadryl 50 mg IV once one hour prior to cath. For his nephropathy, he received fluids prior to cath. His RCA has a 80% proximal lesion and a segment of 80% ulcerated mid disease. He received two BMS of proximal and mid RCA lesions. His LAD has a segment of mid ___ disease, his circumflex has a 70-80% lesion and his ramus has 70% proximal disease. He received 600 mg clopidogrel once and was started on clopidogrel 75 mg. He will take clopidogrel 75 mg for one month ending on ___, and continuing aspirin 81 mg indefinitely. #THROMBOCYTOPENIA: Likely due to cirrhosis and alcohol. Patient has history of thrombocytopenia and was found to be thrombocytopenic at the OSH with platelet count of 50,000. His platelet count on ___ was 52,000 and the cardiologist felt comfortable doing cath at that platelet count without transfusion. #HYPERTENSION: We continued his home medication: amlodipine 10 mg, metoprolol succinate 25 mg and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily #DIABETES II: Patient has poorly controlled diabetes with last HbA1c from ___ of 9.0. He has a diabetic ulcer on his left foot. Is on insulin at home: insulin aspartat( novolog), insulin detemir (levemir). In the our hospital, he was on a insulin sliding scale, reduced dose long-acting while NPO. #DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: We continued his home sertraline 50 mg daily and clonazepam 0.5 mg TID PRN for anxiety. #CHRONIC BACK PAIN/NEUROPATHIC PAIN: We continued his home gabapentin 800 mg TID and he received home oxycodone 10 mg Q6H PRN or oxycodone ___ mg Q4H PRN. #CIRRHOSIS: His cirrhosis is likely secondary to HCV and alcohol. He denied history of prior bleeding. His INR at discharge on ___ was 1.4. #ALCOHOL USE: He received thiamine IV, multivitamins and folate. We strongly recommended alcohol and smoking cessation. #PANCYTOPENIA: Patient was pancytopenic with WBC 1.8, Hgb 10.9 and ___ ___ on ___. Possible causes are chronic splenic congestion, secondary to cirrhosis, myelosupression from chronic alcohol abuse and for thrombocytopenia, ITP due to hepatitis C. We monitored his CBC daily. ## TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: We recommend endoscopy (EGD) as an outpatient to check for varices and assess bleeding risk. We asked patient to call our Gastroenterology Department ___ for an appointment for an endoscopy (EGD).
summary: You initially presented at ___ for chest pain and shortness of breath. Your heart marker in the blood, called troponin, was elevated. In combination with chest pain, this suggests a blockage in one of the vessels, that supply your heart with blood, called coronary vessels. You were also found to have a low platelet count. Your CXR showed that you had some fluid in your lungs, called a mild pulmonary edema. A cardiac ultrasound showed that your heart is pumping well. You received morphine for your pain, a medication that removes fluid from your body, called lasix and were put on a blood thinner, called heparin. You were transferred to our hospital for catheterization.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ F with hx of schizophrenia and recent GI bleed from ulcer at gastric bypass junction who p/w one day of progressive, limiting, lower extremity edema. Active Issues: #Bilateral leg edema: Patient with swelling while inpatient prior that was not this severe. ___ edema for which she's on Lasix at home. Started on Augmentin for possible cellulitis (although thought unlikely, Day 1 of ___ was ___. Negative lower extremity U/S in ED. Her Lasix was increased to 60mg daily and she was given Spironolactone 25mg BID; this, along with leg wraps and elevation and her edema improved. She was discharged at a weight of 106kg and should follow up with her PCP to continue to assess edema and her electrolytes checked. . #Abdominal Pain: Patient with intermittent abdominal pain not related to eating. Sharp pain in URQ. Lasts about 30 minutes and goes away. Initially described as "crampy," similar to when she's having her menses. Was non-tender on exam, labs were not concerning for hepatobiliary process. #Right subclavian dermatitis: Patient with area of dermatitis after R subclavian removed. Appears allergic to the tape used. Low dose steroid was applied to the area with improvement. Was not sent home on the cream because did not want to weaken the skin. #Incontinence: Patient states she has had a recent history of incontinence, most likely stress incontinence per her history. With further difficulty due to her edema, she was prescribed adult diapers for home. Chronic Issues: #S/p GI Bleed- from marginal ulcer at the location of her gastric bypass anastamosis. She had no varices on exam. Patient received her 5 days post GI bleed antibiotics. As an inpatient, she received Pantoprazole 40mg BID. An H. pylori stool antigen was collected, result should be followed up on. No evidence of bleed while she was in house. Iron and folate supplements were continued. #Alcoholic Cirrhosis- LFTs slightly elevated from previous discharge. Patient has denied any use since she left the hospital. LFTs trended down while she was in the hospital. Thiamine and Lactulose were continued. #Depression: Continue Fluoxetine 20mg PO daily #Chronic back pain: Pt with complaints of back pain on previous admission. Ordered for a lidocaine patch and tylenol prn Transition Issues: #Patient should follow up with PCP to see how lower extremity edema is progressing. Her discharge weight was 106kg, still with significant edema. She should also have her labs checked with her PCP since ___ dose was increased on discharge. #S/p GI bleed: Will need to follow up with hepatologist to discuss recent GI bleed from an ulcer at junction of gastric bypass surgery. #H. pylori stool antigen sent to see if she should be treated, although expectation is that the ulcer is a result of poor blood flow to anastamosis. Will need to follow up this result. #Hx of EtOH and drug abuse: Patient states she does not want to abuse substances anymore. She should be encouraged and assisted as an outpatient.
summary: You came to the hospital with swelling in both of your legs that decreased your ability to walk. Using ultrasound, we showed it to be unlikely that you have a clot in your legs. Also, we started antibiotics because you had a fever while you were here, and while that is precautionary, we think it to be unlikely that you are infected. The swelling improved with elevation of your legs, water pills to help you urinate excess fluids, and wraps around your legs. This most likely indicates that your swelling is due to Venous Stasis (a problem with your veins getting blood flow back from your feet). This should continue to improve with compression, elevation, and taking the water pills (Furosemide and Spironolactone). The rest of your labs looked normal while you were here and your liver enzymes were still improving from your previous admission.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Right perioral numbness: Originally thought to be due to progression of large-B cell lymphoma with CNS involvement, however, patient developed a cold sore on her right lower lip so it was more likely due to HSV. Resolved with acyclovir. MRI head was obtained which did not show conclusive evidence of involvement. # Large B-cell lymphoma: Patient had bone marrow biopsy to evaluate for progression of lymphoma. Second cycle of methotrexate was administered. In addition to a bone marrow biopsy, she had a biopsy of an inguinal lymph node. The pathology came back as high grade B cell lymphoma for both the bone marrow and the inguinal lymph node. She received 3 days of ICE chemotherapy, which she tolerated well. She was started on neupogen on the day of discharge. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued simvastatin 20 mg po qdaily. # Asthma: Continued monteleukast 10 mg po qdail. # Depression: Continued citalopram and fexofendine. # GERD: Continued omeprazole.
summary: You were admitted because you had numbness around your right lower lip. It was thought this was due to progression of your large-B cell lymphoma with CNS involvement, however, you developed a cold sore on your right lower lip so it was more likely due to HSV. You were treated with acyclovir and it resolved. MRI of your head was obtained along with bone marrow biopsy to evaluate for progression of your lymphoma. Second cycle of methotrexate was administered which you tolerated well. In addition to a bone marrow biopsy, you had a biopsy of an inguinal lymph node. The pathology came back as high grade B cell lymphoma for both the bone marrow and the inguinal lymph node. You received 3 days of ICE chemotherapy, which you tolerated well. You were started on neupogen on the day of discharge and you should continue giving yourself one injection daily until directed otherwise by your oncologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with metastatic endometrial adenocarcinoma who presented with abdominal cramping and constipation found to have interval progression of intraabdominal tumor burden with mass partially encasing distal sigmoid colon causing partial large bowel obstruction s/p diverting ostomy ___ complicated by worsening abdominal pain, intermittent fevers, and leukocytosis found to have post-op abscess s/p revision and abscess drainage on ___ complicated by difficult to control cancer-related pain. # Cancer-Related Abdominal Pain: # Large Bowel Obstruction: Presented with abdominal cramping and constipation found to have interval progression of intraabdominal tumor burden with mass partially encasing distal sigmoid colon causing partial large bowel obstruction. Staging CT chest without interval change. Gastrograffin enema revealed high grade stricture in distal sigmoid colon and upper rectum at least 5.2 cm in length. She is s/p elective diverting ostomy on ___ and underwent revision on ___. CT with PO contrast shows no obstruction on ___. Palliative Care was consulted for assistance with pain control. Patient discharged on Tylenol 1g TID, ibuprofen 200mg TID, oxycontin 40mg q12h, and oxycodone ___ q4h PRN. Patient also discharged on lorazepam for insomnia and anxiety. Also on dicyclomine. She was counselled extensively on pain medication usage and pain management techniques. Suspect component of pain related to anxiety. Pain was controlled at time of discharge. Medications were delivered to bedside prior to discharge. # Abdominal Wall Abscess: Following initial surgery had fever and leukocytosis found to have abscess s/p cavity drainage. ___ drain removed by surgery on ___. She received 7-day course of cefepime/flagyl. # Metastatic Endometrial Adenocarcinoma: # Secondary Neoplasm of Lung: Previously on tamoxifen/megace. She received carboplatin on ___. Treating with palliative intent. Will follow-up with Dr. ___. # Vaginal Bleeding: # Anemia: Secondary to disease recurrence. She received Ferric Gluconate 125mg IV x 4 doses. # Fatigue in Malignancy: In setting of cancer recurrence, surgery, and infection. Seen by ___ and will have home ___. # Moderate Malnutrition: Due to poor PO intake and weight loss. Seen by Nutrition. Recommended supplements. # Depression: Continued home Celexa. # Insomnia: Discharged on Ativan PRN. # BILLING: 60 minutes were spent in preparation of discharge summary, coordination with outpatient providers, and counselling patient and family. ==================== Transitional Issues: ==================== - Pain Control: Patient discharged on Tylenol 1g TID, ibuprofen 200mg TID, oxycontin 40mg q12h, and oxycodone ___ q4h PRN. Patient also discharged on lorazepam for insomnia and anxiety. Please ensure follow-up with Palliative Care. - Please ensure follow-up with Therapy and consider uptitration of citalopram.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with abdominal pain and constipation. You were found to have a blockage in your large bowel. You had a surgery to relieve the blockage. You then had an abscess around the surgery and this was drained. You completed a course of antibiotics for the infection in the hospital. We also modified you pain medications while in the hospital. Please continue to take the pain medications as indicated. Please keep a log of your pain as well as when and how many oxycodone you are taking. Please bring this log to all of your clinic appointments. For your as needed oxycodone you are written for a range of ___ every 4 hours. For moderate pain, you can take 10mg. For more severe pain, you can take 15mg. Also if after you take 10mg and still having pain, you can take an additional 5mg. You can use the lorazepam for insomnia as well as anxiety. This medication can cause more sedation when taking with oxycodone so please be cautious.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with a history of COPD (intermittent home O2 2L), seizure disorder, frequent falls, HTN, insomnia, who presented with dyspnea for several days despite oxygen, albuterol and spiriva therapy at home. She is also smoking more this month than previously, up to ___ pack per day from ___ pack per day. She is also not taking her Spiriva or Steroids daily. summary: ___ came to the hospital for trouble breathing and frequent falls. A chest XRAY was negative for pneumonia. A CT of your head did not show any damage from your fallsYou were admitted to the hospital for further treatment of your COPDYou were given steroids and an antibiotic to help your shortness of breath. In addition, ___ recieved nebulizer treatments, that helped your breathing significantly. ___ will continue your prednisone and azithromycin for 2 more days after discharge (end date ___You were also provided a nebulizer to start using nebulizer treatments every 6 hours as needed for wheezing, shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with stage III recurrent lung CA s/p chemotherapy and depression who presents with progressive dyspnea and dry cough found to have progression of disease on CXR. # Dyspnea: Initial CXR raised concern for lymphangetic spread of her disease. A CT chest showed progression of her lung cancer, emphysema, peripheral pulmonary fibrosis and possible infection. She was started on ceftriaxone/azithromycin. Pulmonology was consulted, who recommended continuing broad spectrum antibiotics, diuresis, steroids; pt not a candidate for bronch. Her abx were broadened to cefepime/vancomycin/levofloxacin/bactrim. She was started on steroids or possible drug toxicity/COPD flare. She was also diuresed (BNP was elevated, Echo EF 55-60%). There was no evidence of PE on CTA. Despite these interventions her respiratory status worsened, and she progressed to requiring 15L nonrebreather to maintain O2 sats in low ___. With the help of the palliative care team, a family meeting was held where it was decided to transition her to comfort care. She was made CMO, and transitioned to hospice on ___. # Lung CA: s/p s/p C2 Cisplatin/Etoposide, XRT, ___. Palliative Care following. Treatment as above; transition to CMO. # Difficulty urinating: voiding, but has noticed increased difficulty. Possibly ___ morphine; pt discharged to hospice w/ foley catheter. # PE history: Eenoxaparin d/c'd when made CMO. # Depression: sertraline d/c'd when made CMO. #Goals of care. Pt transitioned to CMO and transitioned to hospice on ___ as above. Family in agreement with comfort care.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with cough and shortness of breath. You were given antibiotics to treat a lung infection. You were also given steroids and other medications to help improve your breathing, as well as diuretics to treat whatever fluid might be contributing to your symptoms. Unfortunately your breathing did not improve; we believe your symptoms may represent a progression of your underlying cancer. Even if not, the process has not been responsive to our treatments. We met with your family to discuss your care options, which is what you indicated was your wish (you declined to participate in the meeting).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ====================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] We increased Imdur to 60mg daily [] We also increased diltiazem to 120mg XR daily [] Patient has follow up on ___ with interventional cardiology, as previously scheduled. We will communicate with the interventional team ahead of time to determine options for PCI vs bypass. [] Instructed patient to take acetaminophen 1g Q8 as standing for one week instead of PRN for musculoskeletal injury to left upper quadrant [] Discharged patient also with lidocaine cream 5% for symptomatic relief of musculoskeletal pain ======================= ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with CAD (RCA stent in ___ complicated by restenosis in ___, HTN, DM2, and depression who presents with both exertional and non-exertional chest pain that are separate from one another. # Angina with established CAD Pain is exertional, right-sided, relieved by rest, and lasts a few minutes. He has multi-year history of the same. pMIBI without EKG changes but did show regional WMA. His troponins have been negative but are only 2.5 hours apart. EKG is without ischemic changes. summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had chest pain, both your chronic pain as well as new pain. We examined your heart with a blood tests, EKG, and ultrasound. We found evidence of blood flow that is restricted to a portion of the heart, which is consistent with what we have known from previous procedures back in ___. There is a chance we can fix this, and we would like you to see our interventional cardiologists as an outpatient. You already have an appointment. We will be communicating about your case as well to determine the options. We also addressed pain on your left side, and found no evidence of anything wrong with any of your internal organs. We think this is an injury to the muscle in the area, and we are providing symptomatic treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ yo female with diabetes, HTN, DM, extensive PVD requiring bypass, admitted for cardiac catheterization for unstable angina and positive stress test. She received single DES to RPDA, and balloon rescue of the jailed branch of RPL through radial artery. # Coronary Artery Disease: patient has multiple risk factors for coronary artery disease including HTN, obesity, HLD, tobacco use, PVD, DM, and family history. Her troponins and EKG results were negative for MI at ___. However, her stress test was positive and she was found to have flow limiting lesion of RPDA. She underwent DES to RPDA and PTCA of jailed RPL. She was given integrillin for 12 hours post procedure. She was started on aspirin 81 mg po and clopidigrel 75 mg po daily. She will follow up with Dr. ___ in one month. # Rhythm: Patient was thought to be in atrial fibrillation on an EKG post-procedure. No known hx. No other episodes of atrial fibrillation and she was on normal sinus rhythm for the rest of her hospitalization. Repeat EKG showed normal sinus rhythm. She was started on metoprolol PO 25 mg daily for rate control. Her CHADS2 score=3 but will continue to be on aspirin/plavix for now. She does not wish to start coumadin. Patient has a h/o rheumatic fever when she was ___ years ago. An ECHO was obtained, which showed LVEF>55%, no valves abnormalities, and no change in ventricular function. # Chronic problems: remained stable and she was continued with her home medications -Hypertension: amlodipine, chlorthalidone, losartan -Hyperlipidemia: continue with pravastatin -Diabetes Mellitus Type II: NPH, FSS, sliding scale -peripheral neuropathy: Gabapentin -COPD/asthma: albuterol, fluticasone, ipratropium bromide, adviar -GERD: ranitidine -Depression: bupropion, citalopram -Lumbar back pain: codeine PRN -Neck dermatitis: desonide cream
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You underwent several tests that showed that you were not having a heart attack. However you chest pain was most likely from unstable angina. You had an abnormal stress test and you were referred to ___. We found that you had blockages in your coronary arteries. One stent was placed to open a blockage and one was opened with angioplasty. You will need to take aspirin and plavix for at least one year to keep the stents open. Please do not stop taking these medications without talking to your cardiologist first. You were also started on metoprolol, which is a beta blocker to slow your heart rate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient presented to ED on ___ with a 5 day history of rash that began at his right axilla and subsequently spread to his arms, legs, groin, pannus, and hands. Had been given prednisone and Keflex at outside hospital on ___. Upon presentation, rash was confluent, erythematous and maculopapular in axillae with small papular lesions throughout rest of body causing pruritus. Hands had subcutaneous nodules at knuckles and left great toe was inflamed and painful. Rash: Patient given Salmeterol 125 mg IV and Dilaudid 1mg IV in ED and right axilla skin biopsy performed. Derm concern for ___ ___ prompted admit to floor. Patient was able to ambulate on his own and reported pruritus and left great toe pain upon arrival to floor. Patient given 180 mg daily fexofenadine and 1mg/kg/day prednisone for rash per derm recs and home medications of sertraline and lorazepam held due to concern as causative agents for SJS. Suspicion for SJS decreased upon obtaining results of ___ skin biopsy and dermatology suspected drug reaction with sertraline and lorazepam as likely culprits. Patient given nystatin and miconazole due to suspicion of fungal cause of rash in axillae. Patient reported improvement in pruritus and rash appeared less erythematous. Patient prednisone dose changed to 60 mg daily to be tapered in ___ weeks. Rash continued to improve with decreased erythema and pruritus throughout hospital stay. Patient discharged home on hospital day 3 with prescription for prednisone and fexofenadine and appointments for follow-up care with PCP and dermatology. Steroid-related hyperglycemia/leukocytosis: Lab report on ___ demonstrated elevated glucose level of 280 that trended down to 233 on ___ and 123 on ___. Glucose levels monitored throughout hospital stay to assess potential need for insulin and attributed to high dose steroids. Patient's WBCs were also elevated to 24.5 on ___ which decreased to 19.8 on ___ and was also attributed to high dose steroids. Constipation: Patient given docusate and senna for constipation. Depression/Anxiety: Mental health stable during hospital stay. Patient advised to follow-up with PCP regarding new ___ agents. Restless leg syndrome: Condition stable throughout hospital stay. Continued on home dose of pramipexole. Obstructive sleep apnea: Patient uses a CPAP machine intermittently at home and is unable to tolerate using it consistently. Condition monitored throughout hospital stay without need for CPAP. Asthma: Condition stable throughout hospital stay. Patient continued on home dose of fluticasone-salmeterol, montelukast, and albuterol as needed.
summary: You were admitted for a diffuse rash that was concerning for a possible reaction to medications that you have been taking. Of concern were your usage of Sertraline, Lorazepam, and the recent usage of Keflex. We were unable to determine which medications was the culprit. You were treated with several different steroids during your hospital stay and the rash responded appropriately. You will need to continue the steroids for approximately one month after leaving the hospital. You are not being sent home with either Sertraline or Lorazepam. You should not take any of these medications if you have them at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with Wegener's, hypothyroidism, osteoporosis who presented with hypoxia and stridor from pulmonary clinic felt secondary to Wegener's flare. # Hypoxia. She has progressively worsening Wegener's for which she has required chronic high-dose steroids and Rituxan in addition to frequent admissions for flares. Patient greatly improved throughout admission on high dose steroids (Solumedrol for three days and prednisone 60 mg and ultimately discharged at 40 mg daily). Patient had a total of four bronchoscopies with first one being a flexible bronch demonstrating complex extensive right and left main stem 80% obstruction. She was taken back to the OR three more times for rigid bronchoscopies where the left main bronchus was electrocautarized and then balloon dilated. The right side was much stenosed and fibrosed where dilation would be too risky. The site was injected with Dexamethasone to decrease inflammation and she was taken back to the OR five days later where the right bronchi was successfully dilated. During this procedure, she received an additional dose of succinylcholine. Post-extubation, was noted to have a weak inspiratory effort and LMA placement was attempted but the patient was noted to be bronchospastic and so she was reintubated. She was then admitted to the MICU for further management. She was monitored in the MICU for several hours and was extubated without incident. The remainder of her hospital stay was without incident with good saturations in the high ___ on ___ liters supplemental oxgyen. Patient will continue on high dose steroids with guidance on taper by her pulmonologist in the outpatient. She will be discharged to an acute rehab facility where she can partake in pulmonary rehab as she will require aggressive chest ___. # Wegener's granulomatosis. Followed by Dr. ___. She was continued on bactrim ss ppx, vitamin D and calcium and will continue alendronate in the outpatient. # Weight loss, malnutrition: Patient has had a significant weight loss over the past several months. Nutrition was consulted and felt tube feeds were an option for patient. She refused this. It is advised she drink supplements with each meal as she states she enjoys this. # Memory Loss: Patient became more confused and forgetful during her admission stay. Patient's symptoms were similar to last year when she was on high dose steroids. A full work up was performed last year for this and no other obvious causes of her memory changes were found. She also improved once her steroids were decreased. During this admission her memory problems were felt to be due to high dose steroids. Given the significant benefit this had for her respiratory status these were continued with frequent re-orientation for patient. She understood the risks and benefits of steroids. # Hypothyroidism. TSH within normal limits. Continued levothyroxine # Anxiety/Depression. Started citalopram 20 mg during this admission as patient was felt to be significantly depressed and anxious given her clinical picutre. She was also re-started on diazepam 5 mg BID prn anxiety. She was reportedly on this at home. # Leukocytosis: No signs of infection. Felt secondary to procedure and steroids. This should be repeated in rehab to ensure resolution. # Goals of Care: Given patient tenuous respiratory status and overall clinical picture, goals of care were discussed with both patient and health care proxy. It was decided that patient will be DNR/DNI. She would still want to proceed with bronchoscopies as this is helpful to her comfort and shortness of breath.
summary: You were admitted due to difficutly breathing. You had a procedure called a bronchoscopy that showed significant narrowing of your airways. The lung doctors ___ of your bronchi, or airways. These interventions along with high dose steroids taken orally significantly helped your breathing. You were also noted to have an infection of your trachea called tracheitis. You completed a course of an antibiotic called Augmentin. The pulmonary doctors ___ were safe to go to rehab as your breathing has been quite stable in the hospital. You may need a repeat bronchoscopy in the future and the pulmonary doctors ___ contact ___ for scheduling of this. Also, it was noted that you are not eating enough and are quite malnourished. Tube feeds were recommended, but you declined. You should continue to take liquid supplements with your meals. You had problems with memory during this admission. This was felt secondary to high dose steroids. We decreased your steroids as your respiratory status improved. This will be closely monitored by Dr. ___ in the outpatient.
